proposals.title: Implicit White Identity and Political Utility: A Test of Affective Polarization Beyond Partisanship
proposals.motivation: Research on affective polarization has focused primarily on explicit partisan animosity, yet the persistence of political animosity suggests deeper, identity-based drivers. Pre-2026 work indicates that racial identity is a potent and often unmeasured factor in political behavior and media consumption. This study investigates whether implicit white identity, distinct from explicit partisan identity, serves as a foundational and utility-generating political identity, potentially explaining the durability of polarization and susceptibility to certain information environments.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the strength of implicit white identity, as measured by an Implicit Association Test (IAT), predicts political attitudes and behaviors (e.g., candidate evaluations, policy support, media trust) independently of and more strongly than explicit partisan identity, and that this relationship is moderated by exposure to partisan and racialized news content.
proposals.method: We will design and administer a custom political IAT measuring associations between 'White/Other' and 'Us/Them' or 'American/Un-American' categories to a large, nationally representative online panel. This implicit measure will be embedded within a survey capturing explicit partisan identity, media diet (via validated recall and passive metering data where possible), policy preferences, and affective ratings of political figures. We will use hierarchical regression models to isolate the unique predictive utility of the IAT score, and employ mediation and moderation analyses to test its role in processing racially tinged political misinformation.
proposals.contribution: This project will contribute a novel, psychologically grounded measure of implicit racial-political identity to political communication and behavior research. It will empirically test whether a latent racial identity underpins aspects of modern polarization, offering a more complete explanation for the 'unexpected durability' of political animosity. The findings will inform theories about the intersection of identity, media, and democratic engagement.
proposals.novelty: While IATs are common in social psychology, their application to measure the political utility of a specific racial identity is novel. This moves beyond documenting implicit bias to modeling implicit identity as a political heuristic, bridging psychological measurement with political behavior in a way that existing work on explicit partisan polarization has not.

proposals.title: Agenda Divergence: How Partisan Identity and Media Diet Shape Differential Reception of News Priorities
proposals.motivation: Traditional agenda-setting research often assumes a uniform media agenda is received and adopted by a monolithic public. This assumption is increasingly untenable in a fragmented media ecosystem characterized by high affective polarization and partisan selective exposure. Understanding how news agendas are differentially processed across audience segments is crucial for diagnosing the roots of divergent public priorities and the erosion of a shared democratic reality.
proposals.hypothesis: The perceived salience of issues in the national news agenda varies systematically based on an individual's partisan identity and their primary media diet, with partisan-motivated reasoning leading to the amplification of issues favorable to one's in-party and the attenuation of issues favorable to the out-party.
proposals.method: We will conduct a multi-wave panel survey (N=~3000) during the 2024 U.S. election cycle. Each wave will combine: 1) a content analysis of major broadcast, cable, and digital news outlets using LDA topic modeling to establish a quantified 'media agenda,' and 2) survey measures of respondents' perceived issue importance, partisan identity, affective polarization, and detailed media consumption. We will employ multilevel regression models with cross-level interactions to test how the relationship between the measured media agenda and individual-level agenda salience is moderated by partisanship and media source preference.
proposals.contribution: This project will move agenda-setting theory from a model of uniform reception to a conditional model of partisan and media-diet-based reception. It will provide empirical evidence for 'agenda divergence' as a mechanism underlying polarized perceptions of reality. The findings will directly inform debates about misinformation and democratic resilience by showing how even exposure to overlapping news content can produce divergent public agendas.
proposals.novelty: While prior work by Iyengar and others has documented polarization in news consumption and affective responses, this study uniquely integrates detailed media content data with individual-level panel data to explicitly model and test the *conditional reception* of agenda-setting cues, moving beyond correlations in exposure to causal mechanisms of differential processing.

proposals.title: The Feedback Loop of Affective Polarization: A Vector Autoregression Analysis of Media Tone, Economic Indicators, and Partisan Animosity
proposals.motivation: The persistence of affective polarization in the United States, documented by Iyengar and others, poses a fundamental threat to democratic functioning. While research has established correlations between media content, economic conditions, and public opinion, the dynamic, reciprocal nature of these relationships remains poorly understood. A causal, time-series analysis is needed to disentangle whether negative media drives polarization, polarization drives media negativity, or both are shaped by external shocks like economic performance.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize a self-reinforcing feedback loop exists: increases in negative, partisan media tone directly increase measures of affective polarization, which in turn incentivizes media to produce more negative, partisan content. We further hypothesize that deteriorating real-world economic indicators (e.g., unemployment) will predict increases in both media negativity and polarization.
proposals.method: We will construct a weekly time-series dataset spanning 2015-2024, integrating: 1) media content measures (tonal sentiment and partisan slant from TV and online news transcripts via NLP), 2) public opinion measures (affective polarization from rolling cross-sectional surveys like the ANES and YouGov), and 3) real-world indicators (unemployment, inflation, major political events). We will estimate a Bayesian Vector Autoregression (BVAR) model with Minnesota/Litterman priors to manage parameter proliferation. The model will be identified using a recursive Cholesky ordering, and we will analyze impulse response functions and forecast error variance decompositions to trace dynamic effects.
proposals.contribution: This study will move beyond static correlations to provide the first integrated, dynamic model of the affective polarization system. It will quantify the relative strength of media-driven versus reality-driven polarization and identify the temporal lags in these processes. The findings will inform interventions aimed at breaking this detrimental feedback loop, offering evidence for policymakers and media practitioners.
proposals.novelty: While VAR models are common in macroeconomics, their application to political communication and polarization is novel. This approach uniquely treats media content, public opinion, and real-world conditions as endogenous variables in a single system, allowing us to test for bidirectional causality in a way that cross-sectional or two-wave panel studies cannot.

proposals.title: Agenda Divergence: Measuring the Dynamic Issue Salience of Partisan News Media and Its Impact on Affective Polarization
proposals.motivation: Iyengar's prior work shows persistent affective polarization and the declining information value of broadcast news. However, a key driver of partisan animosity may be the diverging issue agendas set by partisan media, which shape what voters consider important. Pre-2026, large-scale, longitudinal measurement of this agenda-setting divergence across the digital news ecosystem remains methodologically challenging.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the issue salience agendas of left- and right-leaning news outlets have diverged significantly over the past decade, and that this divergence is a stronger predictor of short-term fluctuations in affective polarization than the tone or factual content of coverage.
proposals.method: We will apply a computational linguistics pipeline to a corpus of online news articles (2012-2024) from a stratified sample of partisan outlets. Using BERT-based topic modeling (e.g., BERTopic) and keyword extraction, we will create a dynamic, high-dimensional measure of issue salience for each outlet-week. Time-series analysis (Vector Autoregression) will then model the relationship between agenda divergence metrics, derived from these embeddings, and longitudinal survey measures of affective polarization (e.g., ANES feeling thermometers).
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first large-scale, longitudinal map of partisan agenda divergence in the digital age. It will advance theories of polarization by testing agenda-setting as a mechanism for affective polarization, moving beyond a focus on tone or misinformation. Methodologically, it demonstrates how transformer-based NLP can measure latent media constructs like salience at scale.
proposals.novelty: While existing work analyzes partisan bias in sentiment or misinformation, this project uniquely focuses on quantifying divergence in *what* issues are covered. The application of state-of-the-art (pre-2026) contextual embeddings to measure salience dynamics, and linking them directly to polarization time-series, is a novel integration of computational and political science methods.

proposals.title: The Moralization of the Other: A Computational Text Analysis of Dehumanizing and Polarizing Rhetoric in U.S. Political Discourse (2016-2024)
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 research by Iyengar and others demonstrates that affective polarization and partisan animosity are persistent and corrosive to democratic norms. The rise of misinformation and partisan media ecosystems is implicated, but the specific linguistic mechanisms—particularly the use of 'othering' and moralization language that frames out-groups as threats to sacred values—remain underexplored at scale. Tracking these rhetorical patterns over time is crucial for understanding how elite and media discourse fuels durable animosity.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the prevalence of othering language (e.g., dehumanizing metaphors, exclusionary pronouns) and moral foundation language (particularly related to loyalty, authority, and sanctity) in partisan media and social media has increased since 2016, and that spikes in this language predict subsequent increases in online affective polarization measures.
proposals.method: We will apply a multi-method NLP pipeline to a longitudinal corpus of cable news transcripts, partisan website articles, and Twitter/X data (2016-2024). We will use pre-trained transformer models (e.g., BERT) fine-tuned for sequence classification to identify instances of othering rhetoric. We will also employ established dictionaries, such as the Moral Foundations Dictionary, and use word embedding models (e.g., GloVe) to track semantic shifts in terms associated with partisan identity and threat. Time-series analysis will correlate linguistic trends with external measures of polarization.
proposals.contribution: This project will provide the first large-scale, longitudinal map of how moralized othering language propagates across media tiers, from elite discourse to social media. It will empirically link specific rhetorical strategies to the maintenance of affective polarization, moving beyond broad correlations. The findings will contribute to theories of democratic erosion and the role of communication in shaping partisan identities.
proposals.novelty: While prior work has studied polarization or moral rhetoric separately, this project uniquely integrates these concepts by computationally tracking the fusion of moral and othering language over a critical eight-year period. It also innovates by explicitly modeling the flow of this rhetoric from broadcast/online media to social media, testing a key mechanism for the diffusion of animosity.

proposals.title: Elite Cues and Media Amplification: A Time-Series Analysis of Affective Polarization in the 2024 U.S. Election Cycle
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 research, including Iyengar's own, shows that affective polarization—dislike of the opposing party—remains persistently high and is a core threat to democratic functioning. The specific mechanisms by which elite rhetoric and the fragmented media ecosystem jointly intensify this animus, particularly in real-time during a high-stakes election, are not fully understood. This study directly addresses this gap to inform potential interventions.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that spikes in partisan elite rhetoric (e.g., from candidates and party leaders) will be followed by measurable increases in expressed out-party animosity among the public, and that this relationship will be significantly stronger for individuals embedded in ideologically congruent media ecosystems.
proposals.method: We will employ a dynamic, multi-source time-series design. First, we will collect a daily corpus of elite communications (speeches, social media posts) and cable/online news transcripts from the 2024 election cycle. Using fine-tuned BERT models for sentiment and partisan framing, we will create daily indices of partisan rhetoric intensity. Second, we will field a rolling cross-sectional panel survey (n=~3,000) measuring affective polarization and media consumption. Using vector autoregression (VAR) and time-lagged regression models, we will test the causal sequence and strength of the relationship between elite rhetoric, media coverage, and individual-level animus.
proposals.contribution: This research will provide a fine-grained, temporal map of how elite signals propagate through media channels to shape mass political hostility. It will move beyond documenting static polarization to modeling its dynamic production. The findings will contribute directly to debates on democratic accountability and the role of media in either mitigating or exacerbating partisan conflict.
proposals.novelty: While prior work examines elites or media separately, this project's novelty lies in its integrated, time-sensitive measurement of both supply (elite rhetoric) and curated dissemination (media ecosystems) to explain demand-side (public) affective shifts, using the 2024 election as a natural laboratory.

proposals.title: The Social Architecture of Affective Polarization: A Network Analysis of Partisan Homophily and Cross-Cutting Exposure
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 research by Iyengar and others demonstrates that affective polarization—dislike of the opposing party—is persistent and damaging to democratic discourse. While studies have examined media diets and survey-reported friendships, the actual structure of partisan social networks at scale remains poorly quantified. Understanding the degree of social segregation and the nature of cross-party ties is crucial for diagnosing the roots of political animosity and misinformation diffusion.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that online social networks exhibit strong partisan homophily, creating echo chambers, and that the few cross-party ties that exist are systematically weaker (e.g., lower interaction frequency) and more likely to involve asymmetric information flows, reinforcing rather than bridging partisan divides.
proposals.method: We will construct large-scale ego networks from Twitter (now X) data surrounding the 2024 U.S. election, using user self-reported party affiliation, follower/followee relationships, and interaction metadata (retweets, replies). We will apply community detection algorithms (e.g., the Louvain method) to map partisan clusters and calculate standard network metrics (assortativity, modularity) to quantify segregation. We will then model tie strength and information flow direction across partisan boundaries using exponential random graph models (ERGMs).
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first large-scale, behavioral network map of partisan social ties, moving beyond self-reports to actual interaction patterns. It will quantify the structural underpinnings of the 'echo chamber' and identify the specific characteristics of cross-cutting ties, offering a more precise diagnosis for interventions aimed at reducing polarization and limiting the spread of partisan misinformation.
proposals.novelty: While network analysis has been applied to online politics, this project uniquely integrates it with Iyengar's established work on affective polarization and election-specific dynamics, using behavioral data to test theories about the relationship between social structure and political animosity that have primarily been studied via survey methods.

proposals.title: Exogenous Shocks and the Co-Determination of Media and Public Salience: A Vector Autoregression Analysis of Election Cycles
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 research on media effects and polarization often assumes a unidirectional causal flow, either from media to public opinion or vice versa. This overlooks the possibility that both are jointly responding to external events, potentially misattributing the source of issue salience and polarization. Understanding this co-determination is crucial for diagnosing the health of democratic discourse and the role of media in an era of heightened partisan animosity.
proposals.hypothesis: The observed correlation between media agenda-setting and public issue salience is not primarily a causal relationship, but rather a spurious one driven by their shared, lagged response to exogenous real-world events and developments. This effect will be strongest for 'hard' news topics tied to concrete occurrences.
proposals.method: We will employ a high-frequency time-series Vector Autoregression (VAR) model with exogenous variables (VARX). Using daily data from the 2020 and 2024 U.S. election cycles, we will model media salience (from the Media Cloud database) and public salience (from Twitter/X and Google Trends) for key issues. Exogenous shocks will be operationalized as a time-series of major, discrete real-world events (e.g., Supreme Court rulings, economic reports, geopolitical incidents) identified via structured event data (GDELT). Impulse response functions will trace the dynamic response of both media and public series to these exogenous shocks.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide a more nuanced, empirically grounded model of the media-public ecosystem, moving beyond simple causation. It will quantify the extent to which polarization in issue attention is driven by external events versus endogenous media or social feedback loops. The findings will inform theories of agenda-setting and democratic responsiveness.
proposals.novelty: While VAR models are established, their application to test the co-determination hypothesis against exogenous shocks in political communication is novel. It directly challenges the implicit causal assumptions in Iyengar's prior work on media's declining information value and the diffusion of misinformation.

proposals.title: Agenda-Setting or Public Demand? A Granger Causality Analysis of News Coverage and Issue Salience in a Polarized Era
proposals.motivation: The classic agenda-setting hypothesis posits that media coverage drives public salience of issues, but this relationship is increasingly contested in a fragmented, high-choice media environment. Pre-2026 research by Iyengar and others shows declining information value in broadcast news and persistent polarization, raising critical questions about the direction of influence: does news coverage still set the agenda, or does it increasingly follow pre-existing partisan and public concerns?
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the traditional agenda-setting direction (news → public salience) has weakened for partisan political issues, while a 'public demand' direction (public salience → news coverage) has strengthened, particularly on social media and partisan outlets, reflecting affective and party-driven polarization.
proposals.method: We will construct high-frequency time-series datasets (e.g., daily/weekly) for key issues from 2016-2024. News coverage data will be drawn from TV transcripts (e.g., Vanderbilt Archive) and digital text corpora, coded for volume and sentiment. Public salience data will come from Google Trends, Twitter/X API (pre-2026 access), and repeated survey marginals (e.g., ANES). Using vector autoregression (VAR) models, we will apply pairwise Granger causality tests (with appropriate lag selection via AIC/BIC and tests for stationarity) to statistically assess the direction of influence between each media and public salience series.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide robust, time-based evidence on the evolving dynamics of agenda-setting, directly contributing to political communication and democratic theory. It will clarify whether modern media ecosystems amplify public priorities rather than shape them, with implications for understanding misinformation diffusion and the drivers of persistent polarization.
proposals.novelty: While agenda-setting is well-studied, applying formal Granger causality tests to high-frequency, multi-source data across legacy and digital media to disentangle directionality in the contemporary polarized context is methodologically novel and addresses a core theoretical ambiguity.

proposals.title: Dynamic Agenda-Setting in a Polarized Era: A Vector Autoregression Analysis of Media-Public Feedback Loops
proposals.motivation: The relationship between media agendas and public opinion is central to democratic accountability, yet it is increasingly strained by partisan media ecosystems and declining trust in news. Pre-2026 research documents persistent polarization and the diffusion of misinformation, but often treats media influence as a one-way, static process. Understanding the dynamic, reciprocal feedback between what the media covers and what the public cares about is crucial for diagnosing the health of the public sphere.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that in the contemporary U.S. media environment, the traditional 'media sets the public agenda' model is supplanted by a reinforcing feedback loop, where partisan media agendas and affective partisan responses dynamically amplify each other over time, particularly around elections.
proposals.method: We will apply Panel Vector Autoregression (PVAR) models to high-frequency longitudinal data. The model will treat time-series measures of media agenda (e.g., topic prevalence in cable news transcripts from the Vanderbilt TV News Archive) and public agenda (e.g., most important problem survey responses from Gallup or ANES time-series) as endogenous variables. We will estimate Granger causality, impulse response functions, and forecast error variance decomposition to quantify the direction, magnitude, and persistence of influence between media and public agendas across different partisan subgroups.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide a rigorous, dynamic model of agenda-setting, moving beyond cross-sectional correlations. It will empirically quantify the strength of feedback loops between media and partisan publics, offering new evidence on whether these dynamics entrench polarization. The findings will contribute to theories of democratic responsiveness and the mechanisms of affective polarization.
proposals.novelty: While VAR models are established in macroeconomics, their application to political communication questions about agenda-setting is novel. This approach uniquely allows us to model the *temporal* causality and reciprocal influence between media content and public opinion using observational longitudinal data, a significant advance over standard regression techniques.

proposals.title: Inferring Individual-Level Agenda-Setting in a Fragmented Media Ecosystem: An Ecological Inference Approach
proposals.motivation: The rise of digital media and partisan news sources has fragmented the public agenda, complicating the study of agenda-setting effects. Traditional survey methods are costly and suffer from recall bias, while aggregate network data obscures individual-level exposure and susceptibility. Understanding how media consumption patterns at the individual level translate into issue salience is crucial for diagnosing democratic dysfunction, as highlighted by persistent polarization and misinformation diffusion.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by applying ecological inference models to aggregate data on media outlet agendas and audience demographics, we can recover reliable estimates of individual-level agenda-setting effects, revealing heterogeneous susceptibility across partisan and demographic groups.
proposals.method: We will employ King's Ecological Inference (EI) and its hierarchical Bayesian extensions (e.g., R x C EI) to estimate individual-level parameters from aggregate data. The analysis will combine: 1) a corpus of news content (headlines, lead paragraphs) from a stratified sample of broadcast, cable, and online outlets (2016-2024) to construct outlet-specific issue agendas; 2) aggregated, privacy-preserving Facebook audience data for these outlets, detailing demographic and geographic composition; and 3) aggregate public opinion data on issue salience from large surveys like the ANES. The EI model will treat outlet audience proportions as 'precincts' to infer the probability that an individual in a given demographic group adopts an issue priority after exposure.
proposals.contribution: This research will provide a novel methodological bridge for studying media effects at scale without individual-level tracking data, offering new insights into how partisan media ecosystems differentially shape the perceived importance of issues like election integrity, climate, or the economy. It will directly extend Iyengar's work on polarization and the declining informational value of broadcast news into the digital arena.
proposals.novelty: While ecological inference is established in political science for voting behavior, its application to estimate individual-level communication effects from aggregated digital trace and audience data is novel. This approach moves beyond correlational analyses of network diffusion to model the psychological process of agenda-setting as an inferential problem.

proposals.title: Personalized Agendas: Measuring Agenda-Setting Effects from Individual Media Diets in a High-Choice Environment
proposals.motivation: Traditional agenda-setting research relies on aggregate analyses of major network news, a method increasingly misaligned with today's fragmented media ecosystem. As Iyengar's own work shows the declining information value of broadcast news and the diffusion of (mis)information on social platforms, we lack a framework to measure how personalized media diets shape individual perceptions of issue importance. This gap limits our understanding of how polarization and misinformation are fueled by divergent informational environments.
proposals.hypothesis: The strength of agenda-setting effects is moderated by the partisan composition and source diversity of an individual's media diet, with more homogeneous, partisan-aligned diets leading to stronger agenda-setting but on a narrower, more ideologically congruent set of issues.
proposals.method: We will recruit a nationally representative panel (N=2000) and use a combination of passive metering (via a browser extension) and active self-reports to construct detailed, time-stamped media exposure logs over a 6-month period surrounding a national election. Using pre-2026 NLP techniques (specifically BERTopic for dynamic topic modeling and LIWC for sentiment/frame analysis), we will code the substantive agenda and affective tone of each consumed source. We will then model the lagged relationship between the issue composition of an individual's prior-week media diet and their subsequent survey-reported issue salience, using multilevel models with cross-level interactions for diet homogeneity.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first individual-level, longitudinal test of agenda-setting theory in a high-choice media environment. It will directly quantify how personalized information flows contribute to divergent public agendas, offering a mechanistic link between media fragmentation and persistent polarization. The findings will inform interventions aimed at broadening media exposure to strengthen democratic deliberation.
proposals.novelty: While existing work tracks aggregate media supply or self-reported exposure, this proposal innovates by directly measuring the *consumed* information diet at the individual level and modeling its dynamic effects on salience, moving beyond the assumption of a monolithic public agenda.

proposals.title: Cynicism, Apathy, and Turnout: Disentangling Spurious Correlations in the Political Engagement Link
proposals.motivation: A robust literature, including Iyengar's own work on polarization and declining news value, documents a correlation between political cynicism and reduced voter turnout. This relationship is often interpreted causally, informing interventions in news and democratic practice. However, if this link is spurious—confounded by a latent trait of generalized political apathy—these interventions may be misdirected, failing to address the true barrier to participation.
proposals.hypothesis: The observed negative relationship between political cynicism and voter turnout is not a direct causal effect but is largely or entirely explained by a pre-existing, latent variable of generalized political apathy, which drives both cynicism and abstention.
proposals.method: We will analyze panel survey data (e.g., ANES or CCES) to measure cynicism, internal/external political efficacy, and turnout across multiple election cycles. Using a structural equation modeling (SEM) framework, we will specify apathy as a latent factor indicated by low internal efficacy and high external locus of control. We will then employ causal mediation analysis (using the potential outcomes framework and g-computation) to estimate the average controlled direct effect (ACDE) of cynicism on turnout, conditioning on and controlling for the latent apathy variable, thereby isolating any direct causal pathway.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide a more precise theoretical account of the behavioral mechanism linking political attitudes to action, moving beyond simple correlational claims. It will offer methodological clarity for future work on political engagement and inform more effective, targeted interventions to bolster democratic participation by distinguishing between situational cynicism and dispositional apathy.
proposals.novelty: While prior work treats cynicism as an independent cause of turnout, this project explicitly models the potential for spuriousness by adapting causal inference techniques from epidemiology to test for confounding by a latent, pre-existing trait—a novel application in political communication research.

proposals.title: Counter-Framing and Partisan Resistance: An Experimental and Text-Analytic Study of Active Audience Interpretation of News Frames
proposals.motivation: Research on media effects, particularly framing, often assumes a relatively passive audience. However, in the pre-2026 context of high affective polarization and partisan media ecosystems, this model is increasingly inadequate. Understanding how audiences actively reinterpret or resist frames is crucial for explaining the persistence of polarization and the limited efficacy of fact-checking or corrective information.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that partisan audiences do not passively accept news frames but actively engage in counter-framing, systematically reinterpreting episodic (individual-case) and thematic (broader-context) frames to reinforce their pre-existing ideological commitments and partisan identities.
proposals.method: We will conduct a multi-wave, online survey experiment presenting participants with news vignettes on polarized issues (e.g., poverty, immigration) that systematically vary episodic vs. thematic framing. Using open-ended response prompts, we will collect textual data on how participants interpret the story. We will analyze these responses using a pre-trained transformer model (e.g., BERT) fine-tuned for sentiment and frame detection, combined with dictionary-based methods for identifying counter-arguments and partisan cueing. This will allow us to quantify the direction and intensity of counter-framing as a function of participant ideology, party ID, and media diet.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide direct empirical evidence for an active, interpretive audience model in political communication. It will specify the mechanisms of partisan resistance to frames, moving beyond simple exposure effects. The findings will contribute to theories of why polarization endures despite shared media events and inform interventions aimed at reducing misperceptions.
proposals.novelty: While Iyengar's prior work has documented polarization and framing effects, this project directly tests the *interpretive process* as the missing link, using computational text analysis on open-ended responses to move beyond forced-choice survey items. It integrates experimental design with natural language processing to capture the nuance of audience rebuttal and reinterpretation.

proposals.title: From Anecdote to Action: Testing the Catalytic Power of Episodic Framing on Support for Systemic Policy Solutions
proposals.motivation: Political communication research often posits a dichotomy where episodic frames (individual stories) foster attributions of individual responsibility, while thematic frames (systemic context) foster support for systemic solutions. This leaves a critical gap: in a polarized media environment dominated by affective partisan identities, we lack an understanding of when and why powerful individual narratives might paradoxically catalyze demand for broad, structural policy change. This is crucial for understanding how democratic discourse can bridge partisan divides on complex issues like healthcare or climate policy.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that episodic frames featuring sympathetic, relatable individuals can increase support for systemic policy solutions, but only when the narrative explicitly bridges the individual's plight to a systemic failure and is delivered by a source perceived as non-partisan by the recipient.
proposals.method: We will conduct a series of large-scale, longitudinal survey experiments embedded in a multi-wave panel design around a salient policy issue (e.g., medical debt). Using a 2x2x2 factorial design, we will manipulate frame type (episodic vs. thematic), presence of an explicit systemic bridge in the narrative, and source cue (partisan vs. non-partisan). We will measure immediate affective responses, policy support, and behavioral intentions (e.g., signing a petition). The panel component will track the durability of these effects over time and their diffusion within participants' social networks, employing network analysis techniques to model information flow.
proposals.contribution: This project will directly challenge and refine the classic episodic/thematic dichotomy, providing a more nuanced theory of narrative persuasion. It will contribute to Iyengar's work on affective polarization by testing whether non-partisan, human-centered stories can circumvent partisan motivated reasoning to build consensus for systemic reform. Methodologically, it advances the study of media effects by combining experimental causal identification with longitudinal and network diffusion analyses.
proposals.novelty: The non-obvious claim is that a deeply personal, episodic story—not a broad thematic argument—can be the most effective catalyst for systemic policy support under specific narrative and source conditions, potentially overcoming the persistent polarization observed in Iyengar's prior election studies.

proposals.title: Decoding the Frame: A Multi-Modal Pattern Analysis of Episodic vs. Thematic News Framing on Policy Preferences
proposals.motivation: In an era of persistent political polarization and declining trust in news, understanding how media frames shape policy preferences is critical for democratic health. Pre-2026 research by Iyengar and others shows that episodic (personal) and thematic (systemic) frames have distinct effects, but traditional methods often treat these effects as isolated, failing to capture their integrated cognitive and affective signatures. This limits our ability to predict how framing drives polarization on complex issues like immigration or climate change.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that exposure to episodic versus thematic news frames generates distinct, multi-dimensional response patterns in individuals, encompassing explicit attitudes, implicit biases, and physiological arousal. A machine learning classifier can be trained to accurately decode the frame type from this integrated response signature, revealing the unique constellation of effects each frame produces.
proposals.method: Participants will be exposed to controlled news stories about a policy issue (e.g., poverty), randomly assigned to episodic or thematic framing. We will collect a multi-modal dataset: self-reported policy support, skin conductance response (SCR), facial electromyography (fEMG) for positive/negative affect, reaction times on an Implicit Association Test (IAT), and open-ended thought listings for cognitive responses. Using a Support Vector Machine (SVM) with a radial basis function kernel, we will train a classifier to predict the frame condition from this multi-feature vector, performing feature importance analysis to identify the most diagnostic components of the framing effect.
proposals.contribution: This study will move framing effects research from testing mean differences to modeling the integrated, multi-channel 'signature' of a frame, providing a more holistic and precise account of its influence. It will directly contribute to theories of affective and cognitive polarization by identifying which response pathways are most activated by different narrative strategies, with implications for understanding misinformation diffusion.
proposals.novelty: The approach is novel in adapting the multivariate pattern analysis (MVPA) logic from cognitive neuroscience to a communication science problem, treating the human response to a frame as a decodable neural-like pattern. It integrates disparate measurement traditions (physiological, implicit, explicit) using a predictive modeling framework rarely applied to framing research pre-2026.

proposals.title: Drilling the News Core: A Paleoclimatological Approach to Mapping Long-Term Framing Shifts and Tipping Points in Political Discourse
proposals.motivation: Understanding the long-term dynamics of media framing is crucial for diagnosing the health of democratic discourse, particularly given concerns about persistent polarization and the declining information value of news. Existing studies often focus on short-term campaign effects or single events, lacking the methodological tools to systematically detect cyclical patterns, gradual trends, and abrupt narrative shifts over decades. This gap limits our ability to contextualize contemporary changes in partisan media environments and misinformation diffusion.
proposals.hypothesis: Applying core-sampling and time-series analysis methods from paleoclimatology to longitudinal news archives will reveal distinct, measurable regimes in media framing—characterized by cyclical oscillations, secular trends, and abrupt tipping points—that correlate with major political and technological events.
proposals.method: We will treat a comprehensive digital news archive (e.g., TV News Archive, LexisNexis) from 1990-2024 as a temporal core. Using stratified systematic sampling (e.g., weekly intervals), we will extract article samples for a salient, persistent issue like immigration or climate change. For each sample, we will employ a BERT-based model fine-tuned on the Media Frames Corpus to classify episodic vs. thematic frames and quantify their prevalence ratio as a key 'proxy.' The resulting time-series will be analyzed using Bayesian change-point detection (e.g., Pruned Exact Linear Time algorithm) to identify framing regime shifts and spectral analysis to uncover cyclical patterns.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide a novel, rigorous methodology for longitudinal media analysis, moving beyond case studies to model framing dynamics as a continuous process. It will empirically identify the timing and magnitude of major framing shifts, offering a new lens to examine how external shocks (e.g., elections, crises) and internal media logic reshape public discourse over extended periods.
proposals.novelty: The approach is novel in its direct methodological translation from paleoclimatology to communication science, treating news archives as stratigraphic records and applying geophysical time-series models to cultural data. This moves beyond descriptive content analysis to a hypothesis-testing framework for detecting structural patterns in discourse.

proposals.title: Niche Pressures and Framing Strategies: An Ecological Analysis of Episodic and Thematic Frames Across Print and Television News
proposals.motivation: The persistent differences in how print and television news frame political issues are well-documented but often explained anecdotally. This variation is critical to understanding the fragmented information environment that fuels polarization and shapes public understanding of complex issues like elections and misinformation. A systematic, theory-driven model is needed to explain how the structural properties of each medium act as selective pressures, shaping the 'ecosystem' of available frames.
proposals.hypothesis: Print and television news occupy distinct 'frame niches' characterized by differing niche breadths and minimal overlap. Specifically, television's format exerts pressure favoring episodic, personal frames, while print's format supports a broader niche inclusive of thematic, contextual frames.
proposals.method: We will conduct a synchronized content analysis of print (e.g., NYT, WSJ front pages) and major network/cable TV evening news transcripts for identical political issues (e.g., election integrity, climate policy) over a 6-month period. Using established dictionaries and supervised machine learning (e.g., SVM, logistic regression) for frame classification, we will quantify the proportion of episodic vs. thematic frames per outlet. We will then calculate ecological metrics: niche breadth (Levins' B) for each format's frame diversity and niche overlap (Pianka's index) between formats.
proposals.contribution: This study provides a novel, quantitative framework for modeling media environments, moving beyond descriptive comparisons to test a theory of format-driven selection. It will directly inform debates on the declining informational value of broadcast news and the differential diffusion of (mis)information by linking medium-specific constraints to concrete framing outputs.
proposals.novelty: While niche theory has been applied to media organizations and audience attention, its application to the micro-level competition of cognitive frames within a shared issue environment is a novel theoretical and methodological advance for political communication.

proposals.title: Beyond the Transcript: A Multimodal Computational Framework for Context-Aware Frame Detection in Broadcast News
proposals.motivation: Iyengar's prior work demonstrates the declining information value of broadcast news and the persistent polarization of the American public. Understanding how news frames are constructed and delivered is critical to explaining these phenomena. Current computational frame detection relies almost exclusively on text, ignoring crucial contextual cues from visuals, graphics, and prosody, which limits validity and misses the full rhetorical presentation of news.
proposals.hypothesis: Integrating linguistic, visual, and auditory features from broadcast news videos will yield a more robust, valid, and fine-grained classification of episodic and thematic frames than text-only models, capturing the complete persuasive context that shapes audience perception.
proposals.method: We will build a pipeline to process large-scale news video archives (e.g., Vanderbilt TV News Archive). We will use BERT-based models for transcript analysis, CNN architectures (e.g., ResNet) for on-screen text/graphics detection, and pre-trained audio models (e.g., wav2vec 2.0) for prosodic features. A multimodal fusion architecture (e.g., late fusion or a cross-modal transformer) will integrate these streams. The model will be trained on a human-coded dataset of frames (e.g., episodic/thematic, responsibility, conflict) from key election periods.
proposals.contribution: This project will produce the first validated, scalable system for multimodal frame detection in video news, enabling large-scale longitudinal analysis of framing strategies. It will provide new empirical evidence on how the multimodal presentation of news contributes to affective polarization and the diffusion of political narratives, directly extending Iyengar's work on polarization and misinformation.
proposals.novelty: While multimodal analysis exists, its application to the specific political communication problem of frame detection is novel. The integration of on-screen graphics and speaker prosody as explicit signals for framing moves beyond prior work that fuses only text and generic visual features.

proposals.title: Automated Frame Detection and Agent-Based Simulation of Media-Induced Political Cynicism
proposals.motivation: Research by Iyengar and others shows that media coverage, particularly its framing of political issues, can fuel affective polarization and political cynicism, which in turn suppresses voter turnout. However, existing studies are largely correlational and static, unable to model the dynamic, networked process by which frames propagate and alter collective behavior over time. This gap limits our ability to test interventions in the media ecosystem designed to bolster democratic engagement.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a higher prevalence of thematic (system-focused) frames in news coverage, relative to episodic (individual-focused) frames, drives the propagation of political cynicism through social networks, leading to a measurable decrease in simulated voter turnout in an agent-based model.
proposals.method: We will first build an automated frame detection pipeline using a fine-tuned BERT model on a pre-2026 corpus of news transcripts (e.g., from the Stanford Cable TV News Analyzer) to classify sentences as thematic or episodic. The daily output will generate a 'cynicism dose' metric. This time-series data will parameterize a large-scale agent-based model (built on NetLogo or Mesa) where agents, endowed with attributes from ANES data (efficacy, partisanship), are connected via empirically informed network structures. Agents update internal cynicism based on media exposure and social influence, with turnout as a behavioral outcome.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, dynamic model of media effects that links specific content features to macro-political behaviors via micro-level psychological processes. It will offer a computational laboratory for testing counterfactual media diets and network interventions, directly informing debates about journalistic practice and its role in democratic health.
proposals.novelty: The proposal's novelty lies in the real-time fusion of a state-of-the-art NLP frame classifier with a empirically-grounded agent-based model, creating a closed-loop system to simulate the dynamic, emergent consequences of media framing beyond what standard regression or survey experiments can capture.

proposals.title: Framing the Future: A Multi-Stage Experimental Test of Media Frames on Consequential Responsibility-Taking
proposals.motivation: Research on media effects and polarization often relies on self-reported attitudes or low-cost behavioral intentions, which may not predict real-world, costly actions. This gap is critical in democratic contexts, where media framing of issues like climate change or inequality is hypothesized to influence public willingness to bear personal costs for collective benefit. A method that causally links media exposure to observable, consequential behavior is needed to move beyond attitudinal measures.
proposals.hypothesis: Exposure to media frames emphasizing collective responsibility and efficacy (vs. individual blame or systemic intractability) will increase participants' engagement in costly, delayed, and socially observable prosocial actions, and this effect will persist over time and across different behavioral domains.
proposals.method: We will conduct a longitudinal online experiment using a pre-registered, 2x2 factorial design (frame: collective efficacy vs. individual blame; issue: e.g., climate adaptation). Participants will first be exposed to professionally produced video news segments manipulating the frame. They will then complete a series of incentivized, multi-stage games over several weeks: a binding pledge of future bonus earnings to a relevant charity, an irreversible allocation from a participation endowment, and a costly information-acquisition task about the issue. We will track behavioral persistence and use ANCOVA and mixed-effects models to isolate the causal effect of the frame on the costliness and timing of actions.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide a novel behavioral measure of 'responsibility-taking' that is consequential and ecologically valid, directly testing the real-world impact of media frames. It will extend Iyengar's work on polarization and misinformation by showing how framing affects not just attitudes but tangible, costly behavior crucial for democratic problem-solving. The multi-stage design will offer insights into the durability of media effects beyond immediate exposure.
proposals.novelty: While past work examines media effects on donations or intentions, this design uniquely combines controlled framing with a sequence of incentivized games requiring irreversible, future-oriented, and effortful actions, creating a more robust behavioral assay of responsibility that incorporates uncertainty and social observability.

proposals.title: Disentangling Narrative Diffusion: A Causal Inference Framework for Media Frame Propagation
proposals.motivation: Research on media ecosystems and polarization often assumes that the co-occurrence of frames across outlets signals direct influence or diffusion. However, this correlation could be spurious, driven by shared responses to external events (e.g., economic shocks, press releases) or latent partisan biases. Disentangling true propagation from these confounders is critical for accurately diagnosing the flow of (mis)information and the roots of persistent polarization in democratic discourse.
proposals.hypothesis: A significant portion of observed frame correlation in media networks is not due to direct outlet-to-outlet influence but can be explained by shared latent drivers; applying causal inference methods will reveal a sparser, more accurate network of genuine narrative propagation.
proposals.method: We will construct a dynamic, time-stamped network of frame co-occurrence from a large corpus of news text (e.g., TV transcripts, online articles) around focal events like elections. To estimate causal influence, we will apply econometric panel data methods, such as the two-way fixed effects estimator, to control for time-varying confounders and outlet-specific biases. We will also identify and utilize potential instrumental variables (e.g., geographic proximity to an event, exclusive reporter access) and implement Granger-causality tests within a vector autoregression framework to infer directionality and strength of influence pathways.
proposals.contribution: This project will provide a more robust, causally-grounded model of how political narratives and frames actually spread, moving beyond correlational claims. It will directly inform debates about media influence, echo chambers, and misinformation by quantifying the probability of independent adoption versus direct propagation. The output causal network will be a novel tool for analyzing the integrity of information ecosystems.
proposals.novelty: While network analysis of media content is common, this framework uniquely integrates causal inference techniques from econometrics to rigorously separate direct influence from spurious correlation, addressing a fundamental identification problem in communication research that existing methods like simple time-series correlation cannot solve.

proposals.title: Modeling Narrative Contagion: An SIR Framework for Frame Diffusion and Resistance in the U.S. Partisan Media Ecosystem
proposals.motivation: The diffusion of partisan and misleading frames through media networks is a core driver of political polarization and democratic dysfunction, as shown by Iyengar's work on persistent animosity and misinformation on Facebook. Current research maps static networks but lacks dynamic models to predict the spread and durability of narrative frames, limiting our ability to understand thresholds for virality or design effective counter-messaging.
proposals.hypothesis: The diffusion of partisan narrative frames through a network of media outlets follows dynamics analogous to an epidemic, where the likelihood of a frame becoming endemic depends on network structure, ideological homophily, and the development of audience resistance.
proposals.method: We will construct a directed network of major U.S. news outlets and influential commentators using hyperlink/citation data and shared audience metrics from pre-2026 sources. For a specific contentious frame (e.g., election integrity), we will code outlet states (Susceptible, Infected, Resistant) over time. We will adapt an SIR model, parameterizing transmission (β) using edge weights and ideological similarity, and recovery (γ) using the emergence of corrective or counter-frames. The model will be simulated and validated against observed diffusion patterns from the 2020 and 2024 election cycles.
proposals.contribution: This research will provide a novel, dynamic model for predicting narrative spread, moving beyond descriptive network analysis. It will offer quantifiable estimates for narrative 'herd immunity' and identify critical intervention points for fact-checking or counter-messaging, directly informing debates on media and democratic resilience.
proposals.novelty: While epidemiological analogies are used informally, this project formalizes a compartmental model with parameters derived from political communication variables (ideology, audience overlap), creating a testable, predictive framework for information contagion.

proposals.title: Mapping the Architecture of Framing: A Network Analysis of Frame Diffusion in the 2024 U.S. Election Media Ecosystem
proposals.motivation: The increasing fragmentation and polarization of the media environment, documented in Iyengar's prior work, has profound implications for democratic discourse. Understanding how specific frames—interpretive narratives about political issues—originate and spread across outlets is critical for diagnosing the flow of (mis)information and the roots of affective polarization. Pre-2026 research has tracked content volume and sentiment, but lacks a systematic, structural model of the pathways through which frames propagate.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that frame diffusion follows a core-periphery network structure, where a small set of elite partisan commentators and digital-native outlets act as frame originators, with traditional broadcast and print media occupying distinct, downstream positions in the propagation network, reinforcing partisan alignment in framing.
proposals.method: We will collect a large corpus of text and transcript data from a stratified sample of national broadcast networks, major newspapers, and prominent online commentators/podcasters during the 2024 U.S. election cycle. Using validated, scalable frame dictionaries (e.g., from the Comparative Agendas Project) and NLP techniques like structural topic modeling and keyword-in-context analysis, we will identify the emergence and usage of specific frames. We will then construct temporal, directed networks where nodes are outlets and edges represent the inferred flow of frames based on publication timing and semantic similarity, analyzed using algorithms like the Information Pathways (InfoPath) model and dynamic community detection.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first large-scale, longitudinal network map of frame diffusion in a U.S. election, moving beyond descriptive accounts to a testable model of information flow. It will directly contribute to theories of asymmetric polarization and agenda-setting in a digital media era, and offer a methodological blueprint for tracking narrative propagation.
proposals.novelty: While network science has been applied to diffusion of URLs or hashtags, applying it to the diffusion of complex *interpretive frames* within professional media content is a novel synthesis of political communication theory and computational social science. It moves the unit of analysis from the fact-checkable claim to the more subtle and powerful political narrative.

proposals.title: Mapping the Editorial Cascade: A Temporal Transcriptomics Approach to Frame Dynamics in Broadcast News
proposals.motivation: Political communication research has established the power of media frames to shape public opinion, yet our understanding of how these frames are constructed and evolve *within* a single news narrative remains static. Pre-2026 work on polarization and misinformation highlights the declining informational value of broadcast news and the affective power of partisan media, but lacks a fine-grained, dynamic model of the editorial process itself. This gap limits our ability to understand the internal logic of news production and its real-time implications for democratic discourse.
proposals.hypothesis: Applying a temporal transcriptomics methodology to a single news broadcast will reveal a structured, non-random 'cascade' of narrative frames, where the introduction and dominance of specific frames exhibit temporal dependencies and competitive dynamics analogous to gene expression pathways.
proposals.method: We will select a major U.S. network's prime-time news broadcast covering a salient political event (e.g., a presidential debate or election night). The broadcast will be segmented into a dense time series (e.g., 2-minute intervals). For each segment, we will employ a multi-dimensional coding scheme using a combination of supervised machine learning (e.g., BERT-based classifiers fine-tuned on existing frame lexicons) and human annotation to quantify dominant frames, source diversity, emotional valence (using pre-2026 sentiment lexicons like VADER or LIWC), and metaphorical density. The resulting time-series data will be analyzed using techniques from computational biology, such as clustering (k-means, hierarchical) to identify frame 'states' and Granger causality tests to model temporal dependencies between frames.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first dynamic, process-oriented model of frame construction within a broadcast, moving beyond aggregate content analysis. It will contribute a novel methodological toolkit for analyzing narrative evolution in real-time media. The findings will offer empirical insight into the 'how' of news production, informing debates about editorial decision-making, narrative coherence, and the potential for affective priming within a single program.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the direct methodological analogy from molecular biology to media studies and the micro-temporal analysis of a unified editorial product. Unlike cross-sectional or comparative studies, this approach treats the broadcast as a single, time-bound biological system responding to a stimulus, revealing its internal regulatory dynamics.

proposals.title: Framing the Storm: A Micro-Temporal Analysis of Partisan Framing in a Single Major Election Night Broadcast
proposals.motivation: Prior work on media effects and polarization often aggregates across outlets or time, potentially obscuring the dynamic, moment-by-moment construction of partisan narratives. As broadcast news faces declining informational value but retains significant influence, understanding how a single influential program builds a narrative frame in real-time is crucial for unpacking the mechanisms of affective polarization and elite-driven information flows.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a single prime-time election night broadcast will exhibit significant, systematic shifts in partisan framing (e.g., emphasis on procedural fairness, candidate competence, voter legitimacy) across its timeline, and that these shifts will correlate strategically with incoming electoral results to reinforce in-group/out-group affective evaluations.
proposals.method: We will select a single, highly-rated prime-time election night broadcast (e.g., Fox News or CNN coverage of the 2024 U.S. presidential election). Using a pre-2026 transformer-based architecture (e.g., BERT) fine-tuned on existing frame lexicons (e.g., from the Media Frames Corpus), we will perform automated frame detection on transcribed text at one-minute intervals. We will supplement this with manual coding for affective tone (positive/negative) directed at partisan actors. Time-series analysis and changepoint detection algorithms (e.g., PELT) will identify significant shifts in framing composition over the broadcast's duration.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide a novel, high-resolution map of how partisan narratives are assembled in real-time during a critical democratic event. It will contribute to theories of media priming and affective polarization by demonstrating the processual nature of framing. The methodology offers a blueprint for micro-temporal content analysis applicable to studying misinformation diffusion and broadcast strategy.
proposals.novelty: This approach moves beyond cross-sectional or aggregate comparisons to treat a single broadcast as a dynamic, evolving narrative system, linking specific framing shifts directly to the event timeline—a granularity largely absent in pre-2026 political communication research.

proposals.title: Inferring the Frame: A Bayesian Model of Journalistic Narrative Selection Driven by Public Responsibility Priors
proposals.motivation: Media framing powerfully shapes public understanding of political issues, yet the process by which journalists select specific frames from complex events remains underspecified. In an era of heightened polarization and declining trust in news, understanding the systematic drivers of frame selection is crucial for diagnosing narrative bias and its democratic consequences. This research addresses a core gap by proposing a formal cognitive mechanism linking public sentiment to media content.
proposals.hypothesis: Journalists' selection of responsibility-attributing frames (e.g., blaming a policy failure on the president vs. Congress) can be modeled as Bayesian inference, where the likelihood is the event's factual elements and the prior is the pre-existing distribution of public attributions of responsibility for that issue domain. The stronger and more consensual the public prior, the higher the probability journalists will select the corresponding frame.
proposals.method: We will construct a computational model using a Naive Bayes classifier, treating frame selection as a multinomial classification task. Input features will include: (1) quantified event data (e.g., actor identities, policy domain) and (2) measured 'public priors' derived from time-lagged survey data (e.g., ANES, YouGov) on responsibility attributions. The model will be trained and tested on a corpus of news stories (from LexisNexis/NewsBank) about predefined issue domains (e.g., economic downturn, foreign crisis) from 2010-2024, with frames hand-coded for responsibility attribution. We will compare the predictive power of models with and without the public prior feature.
proposals.contribution: This work provides a formal, testable mechanism for how public sentiment shapes media narratives, moving beyond correlational studies. It contributes to political communication theory by integrating concepts from computational cognitive science. Practically, it offers a model to quantify the 'pull' of public opinion on journalistic decision-making, informing debates about media independence and responsiveness.
proposals.novelty: While framing and priming are well-studied, modeling frame selection itself as a Bayesian inference problem solved by journalists is a novel theoretical synthesis. It inverts the typical direction of influence (media->public) to formally model how public sentiment (as a prior) constrains media output, applying a perceptual cognitive model to a high-level editorial process.

proposals.title: Engineering Engagement: A Multimodal Framing Analysis of Partisan News Narratives in Short-Form Video
proposals.motivation: The rapid shift of news consumption to short-form video platforms like TikTok and Instagram Reels represents a critical frontier for political communication and polarization. Pre-2026 research, including Iyengar's work on declining broadcast news value and online misinformation diffusion, has yet to systematically analyze how the unique multimodal grammar of these videos—combining visuals, audio, text, and rapid editing—engineers persuasive frames for algorithmic amplification. Understanding this holistic construction is essential to assess its impact on political learning and affective polarization in digital environments.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that partisan short-form news videos employ distinct, synergistic combinations of visual, auditory, and editorial framing devices, and that these multimodal packages are systematically associated with higher platform engagement metrics (likes, shares) and viewer affective responses compared to content relying on traditional textual or verbal framing alone.
proposals.method: We will collect a stratified sample of news videos from TikTok, Instagram Reels, and YouTube Shorts surrounding a salient political event (e.g., the 2024 U.S. election). Building on manual framing analysis and computer vision techniques (e.g., OpenCV for visual element detection), we will develop a structured codebook to annotate frames across modalities: visual (imagery, source appearance), audio (music tone, sound effects), textual (on-screen captions), and editorial (cut frequency, transitions). We will use natural language processing (BERT-based models) to analyze spoken/textual narrative frames and time-series analysis to model the interaction of modalities. Engagement metrics and linked survey experiments will test effects on perceived credibility and partisan affect.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first integrated model of how political frames are constructed in the dominant format of algorithmically-driven news. It will extend framing theory into the multimodal, high-engagement platform context and offer empirical evidence on the production strategies that fuel partisan engagement. The resulting framework will be a vital tool for scholars studying misinformation, selective exposure, and the changing nature of democratic discourse.
proposals.novelty: While existing work analyzes text or video content separately, this project's novelty lies in its systematic dissection and integration of the distinct but interacting persuasive codes—visual, auditory, editorial—that define the short-form video format, moving beyond a unimodal focus to capture its holistic rhetorical strategy.

proposals.title: Incidental Exposure in the Digital Backchannel: Measuring News Reach Among the Politically Inattentive
proposals.motivation: Research on political communication and polarization often assumes that citizens with low interest in politics are effectively disconnected from news, creating a 'bypass' effect. However, this assumption may be outdated in a digital media ecosystem where news fragments circulate through social feeds, messaging apps, and algorithmic recommendations. Understanding if and how incidental exposure reaches these low-interest groups is crucial for assessing the health of democratic information environments and the true reach of (mis)information.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that politically inattentive individuals are not completely bypassed by news but receive substantive exposure to political information incidentally through digital social networks and algorithmic curation, and that this exposure is associated with measurable, though limited, changes in political awareness and affective polarization.
proposals.method: We will conduct a two-wave panel survey of U.S. adults, oversampling low political interest individuals, before and after the 2024 election. The survey will integrate passive data collection via a custom browser extension and mobile app to log URLs and social media content viewed. We will use natural language processing (specifically BERT-based topic modeling and named entity recognition) to classify exposure to political news and misinformation from the logged data. This will be combined with network surveys to map respondents' digital social ties and experimental modules to test recall and affective responses to incidentally encountered content.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first comprehensive, behavioral measure of incidental news exposure among the politically inattentive, moving beyond self-reports. It will directly test the 'bypass' assumption central to theories of political communication and polarization. The findings will refine models of information diffusion and have implications for understanding the baseline reach of corrective information and misinformation.
proposals.novelty: While prior work by Iyengar and others has tracked news consumption and polarization, this project inverts the core assumption of complete bypass. It innovates by using passive digital trace data linked to survey measures to empirically test the reach and effects of incidental exposure, a mechanism previously inferred but not directly measured at the individual level for this population.

proposals.title: Mapping the Architecture of Affective Polarization: A Network Analysis of Shared Media Content in U.S. Elections
proposals.motivation: The U.S. media environment is characterized by high-choice, fragmented ecosystems that are theorized to reinforce partisan animosity and affective polarization. While Iyengar's prior work has documented the persistence of this animosity, the precise structural connections between media outlets that facilitate and reflect these polarized information flows remain inadequately mapped. Understanding these linking and sharing networks is crucial for diagnosing the health of the democratic information sphere, especially following the 2020 election's misinformation challenges.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the network of shared content between media sources will reveal distinct, densely connected ideological clusters with asymmetric bridging patterns, where mainstream sources are cited across clusters, but partisan sources exhibit high in-group loyalty and low out-group connectivity, structurally mirroring patterns of affective polarization.
proposals.method: We will collect a large corpus of URLs shared on Facebook and Twitter (via public APIs) during the 2024 U.S. election cycle, identifying the primary domain of each linked news article. Using this data, we will construct a directed, weighted network where nodes are media domains and edges represent the frequency of co-sharing by the same users or pages. We will apply community detection algorithms (e.g., the Leiden algorithm) to identify ideological clusters and use measures of modularity, reciprocity, and centrality (e.g., betweenness) to characterize the network's segregation and the brokerage role of specific outlets.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide a novel, structural map of the contemporary media ecosystem, moving beyond audience or survey-based measures to directly observe the connective tissue of information diffusion. It will contribute to theories of polarization by testing whether affective divides are embedded in the architecture of media sharing itself and offer empirical benchmarks for assessing the reach and containment of misinformation within specific network clusters.
proposals.novelty: While prior network studies often focus on social connections between users, this project innovates by applying network analysis to the *content sources* themselves as the unit of analysis, derived from behavioral sharing data. This bridges the study of media ecosystems with the computational analysis of digital trace data in a way that directly tests theories of partisan information silos.

proposals.title: The Supply-Side of Polarization: A Computational Analysis of Media Content Tailoring in Anticipation of Audience Demand
proposals.motivation: Research on political polarization has extensively documented audience demand for partisan content and its effects, such as affective polarization. However, the supply-side dynamics—how media producers strategically tailor content in anticipation of this selective demand—remain less understood. This gap is critical in the pre-2026 media landscape, where producers, from cable networks to digital outlets, operate with sophisticated data on audience preferences, creating a feedback loop that may accelerate polarization and misinformation.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that media producers systematically adjust the partisan slant, emotional tone, and topic selection of their content in the lead-up to major electoral events, and that these adjustments are predictive of subsequent audience engagement metrics, indicating a strategic anticipation of demand.
proposals.method: We will employ a multi-method computational approach. First, we will collect a large corpus of text and video transcripts from a stratified sample of national and partisan media outlets (e.g., Fox News, MSNBC, NYT, Breitbart) across the 2024 U.S. election cycle. Using validated, pre-2026 NLP tools (e.g., supervised classifiers for partisan slant, LIWC for affective language, BERTopic for thematic analysis), we will quantify content features over time. We will then model these features as a function of lagged audience metrics (e.g., prior-period social media engagement, ratings data) and upcoming electoral events using time-series regression and Granger causality tests to establish anticipatory behavior.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first large-scale, longitudinal evidence of strategic content supply as a driver of political polarization, moving beyond demand-side explanations. It will contribute a formal model of the media-audience feedback loop, offering empirical grounding for theories of partisan media ecosystems and their implications for democratic discourse.
proposals.novelty: While existing work tracks audience selection or static content bias, this project uniquely analyzes *dynamic, anticipatory* content adjustment by suppliers, treating media output as a time-varying strategic variable rather than a fixed trait, which is a novel application of computational methods to a core political communication problem.

proposals.title: The Medium and the Message: A Comparative Experimental Test of Visual Format and Narrative Structure on Political Information Processing and Polarization
proposals.motivation: Research on political communication has long debated the effects of episodic (event-focused) versus thematic (context-focused) news framing on audience perceptions. Concurrently, the shift towards visual and social media has raised concerns about the declining informational value of news. However, it remains unclear whether the *structural format* of information (e.g., dense text vs. visual summary) exerts a stronger influence on comprehension, affective polarization, and misinformation susceptibility than its *narrative framing*. This is critical for understanding the mechanisms behind persistent polarization in a fragmented media environment.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the visual-textual format of a news presentation (e.g., video infographic vs. text article) will have a larger effect on information retention, affective partisan reactions, and perceived credibility than whether the content is framed episodically or thematically.
proposals.method: We will conduct a 2x2 online survey experiment (N=~2000, nationally representative) manipulating Narrative Structure (Episodic vs. Thematic) and Presentation Format (Visual-Dominant vs. Text-Dominant) for identical policy stories. Dependent variables will include information recall tests, feeling thermometers towards partisan groups, and credibility ratings. We will use OLS regression with interaction terms to test the main and interactive effects of format and structure, controlling for party ID, media diet, and digital literacy. A follow-up wave will measure the durability of any effects.
proposals.contribution: This study will directly contribute to theories of media effects by disentangling the influence of presentational medium from narrative content. It will provide empirical evidence for whether concerns about declining news value are more tied to format shifts than to journalistic framing choices. The findings will inform debates on platform design and media literacy in democratic discourse.
proposals.novelty: While prior work examines format or framing in isolation, this proposal innovatively pits these two major dimensions of news construction against each other in a causal experimental design to determine their relative explanatory power for key democratic outcomes.

proposals.title: Thematic Decline and Episodic Spikes: A Computational Analysis of News Framing and Its Role in Political Polarization (2000-2024)
proposals.motivation: Iyengar's prior work shows declining substantive information in broadcast news and persistent political animosity. A key theoretical mechanism linking these phenomena is the shift from thematic framing (context-rich, policy-focused) to episodic framing (event-driven, personality-focused), which is known to reduce attribution of societal responsibility and may fuel affective polarization.
