Quantifying this shift at scale across the digital news landscape is critical for understanding the changing informational foundations of democracy.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that over the past two decades, major U.S. news outlets have exhibited a significant decline in thematic framing and a corresponding increase in episodic framing, particularly around elections, and that this trend is more pronounced in partisan media, correlating with measures of affective polarization in the public.
proposals.method: We will apply supervised machine learning and transformer-based NLP to large corpora (e.g., TV News Archive, GDELT, NewsBank). Using a fine-tuned BERT model, we will classify sentences/stories as episodic or thematic based on established coding schemes. Time-series analysis will track framing proportions across outlets (broadcast, cable, digital) and party-aligned sources. We will correlate framing trends with longitudinal survey data on partisan affect and trust, using vector autoregression to assess temporal relationships.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first large-scale, longitudinal evidence of framing shifts across the modern media ecosystem, directly testing a key mechanism for declining political information quality. It will extend Iyengar's work on polarization by linking macro-level content patterns to micro-level attitudinal outcomes, offering a computational foundation for media effects theories in a fragmented information environment.
proposals.novelty: While framing is a classic concept, its systematic measurement over decades and across diverse media formats using state-of-the-art NLP is novel. The explicit integration of these computational content metrics with long-term affective polarization data moves beyond descriptive analysis to test causal pathways.

proposals.title: Selective Gaze: An Eye-Tracking Study of Partisan Visual Attention to Framed News Content
proposals.motivation: Research on partisan selective exposure and affective polarization demonstrates that individuals consume and process information differently based on their political identity. However, prior work, including Iyengar's own studies on the declining information value of news and persistent polarization, has relied on self-report or behavioral data (e.g., clicks, shares). This leaves a critical gap in understanding the real-time, subconscious cognitive engagement with news frames, which is essential for explaining the durability of polarized beliefs.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that partisan identity will systematically bias visual attention, such that viewers will spend significantly more time fixating on story elements (e.g., specific actors, statistics, images) that are congruent with their partisan priors or that trigger affective responses, regardless of the overall story frame.
proposals.method: We will conduct a controlled lab experiment using a remote eye-tracker (e.g., Tobii Pro Nano). Participants (N=200, balanced by party ID) will view a series of news-style articles on politically salient topics (e.g., inflation, immigration) presented on-screen. Each article will contain multiple visual elements (headline, lead image, pull-quote, data graphic, opposing-source quote). We will employ a mixed-design ANOVA to analyze fixation duration and saccade paths as dependent variables, with partisan congruence of elements and overall story valence as independent variables. Attention heatmaps will be generated for qualitative analysis of gaze patterns.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first direct, physiological evidence of partisan selective attention *within* a single news story, moving beyond channel choice to micro-level information processing. It will contribute to theories of motivated reasoning and the mechanisms of framing effects. The findings will also inform the design of more effective communication strategies and platform interventions aimed at mitigating bias.
proposals.novelty: While eye-tracking is established in media psychology, its application to test theories of partisan polarization in news consumption is novel. It moves the field from measuring exposure choices to measuring the subconscious allocation of attention during exposure, offering a more precise mechanism for how framing and partisan animosity interact.

proposals.title: The Blame Network: Mapping Affective Polarization Through the Structure of Causal Attributions in Political News
proposals.motivation: In the context of rising affective polarization and misinformation, understanding how citizens assign blame for societal problems is crucial for democratic health. Pre-2026 research, including Iyengar's work on persistent polarization and the declining information value of news, suggests media narratives shape these attributions, but the systemic structure of blame—who blames whom and how these accusations cluster—remains unmapped. This gap limits our ability to diagnose the networked foundations of political animosity.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that public attributions of responsibility form distinct, polarized network structures, where in-group and out-party actors are central nodes, and that exposure to different media ecosystems (e.g., Fox News vs. MSNBC audiences) significantly predicts an individual's position within these blame networks.
proposals.method: We will collect data using a survey-embedded experiment and social media analysis. Participants will be presented with salient political issues (e.g., inflation, immigration) and asked to assign causal responsibility to a predefined set of actors (e.g., President, opposing party, media, corporations). We will construct bipartite actor-blame networks for the full sample and for subgroups defined by partisanship and media diet. Using community detection algorithms (e.g., Louvain method) and centrality measures (eigenvector, betweenness), we will identify clusters and key influencers in the blame structure. Regression models will test the effect of media exposure on network position.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first empirical map of the 'blame network' in American politics, quantifying a key mechanism of affective polarization. It will extend network analysis from the diffusion of misinformation to the structure of political cognition, offering a novel diagnostic tool for the resilience of partisan animosity between elections.
proposals.novelty: While network analysis has been applied to information diffusion on platforms like Facebook, applying it to model the cognitive structure of responsibility attributions—treating blame as a connective tissue of polarization—is a novel synthesis of political psychology and computational social science.

proposals.title: Local Frames, National Divides? The Framing of Community Issues in Local News and Its Relationship to Affective Polarization
proposals.motivation: Research on political polarization and media framing has focused overwhelmingly on national politics, despite evidence that local news consumption can reduce affective polarization. However, the decline of local news and its potential shift toward more partisan framing of local issues (e.g., zoning, school boards, policing) remains understudied. This project investigates whether the partisan framing models observed in national news are now being applied to local community problems, potentially extending political animosity into non-partisan civic spheres.
proposals.hypothesis: Local news outlets, particularly those owned by larger partisan media chains, will employ distinct partisan frames (e.g., conflict, responsibility attribution, moral foundation) when covering ostensibly non-partisan local issues, and exposure to these framed stories will increase affective polarization toward co-partisans and out-partisans at the local level.
proposals.method: We will conduct a computational content analysis of local news articles (from digital outlets and newspapers) in 50 demographically diverse U.S. counties from 2020-2024. Using a BERT-based fine-tuned model for frame detection (building on prior work by Boydstun et al. and Entman), we will classify frames in coverage of three key local issues: public school curricula, affordable housing, and infrastructure spending. We will link outlet ownership data (via the Columbia Journalism Review database) to test for framing differences. A subsequent survey experiment will expose participants to differently framed local stories to measure effects on trust in local officials and affective polarization.
proposals.contribution: This study will extend theories of media framing and affective polarization into the critical but neglected domain of local civic life. It will provide the first large-scale, longitudinal evidence on how local issues are framed in the digital age and empirically test whether such framing drives polarization at the community level, informing debates about local news sustainability and democratic health.
proposals.novelty: While Iyengar's prior work has tracked polarization around national elections, this project innovatively shifts the unit of analysis to local community issues, testing whether the mechanisms of partisan animosity operate in the same way when the subject matter is theoretically non-ideological. It also integrates computational text analysis of local media ecosystems with experimental causal evidence.

proposals.title: Narrative Blending and Political Engagement: How Hybrid Episodic-Thematic News Shapes Information Processing and Affective Polarization
proposals.motivation: The decline in substantive policy content in broadcast news and the persistent rise of affective polarization are central challenges to democratic discourse. Prior work shows episodic (personal) framing often reduces attribution of systemic responsibility, while thematic (systemic) framing can feel abstract, potentially limiting engagement. The increasing prevalence of hybrid narratives in digital news, which blend these frames, presents an unexamined vector for how citizens process political information and form attitudes.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that exposure to hybrid episodic-thematic news stories, compared to purely episodic or purely thematic stories, will increase perceived news credibility and recall of policy information, but will also differentially influence affective polarization depending on the partisan alignment of the personal story subject.
proposals.method: We will conduct a multi-wave, nationally representative survey experiment during the 2024 U.S. election period. Participants will be randomly assigned to read professionally produced news articles on salient issues (e.g., economic insecurity) varying in narrative frame: episodic, thematic, or hybrid. We will measure immediate outcomes (recall, perceived credibility) and, using a panel design, track durable effects on affective polarization (feeling thermometers), perceived issue importance, and blame attribution over time. Analysis will employ OLS regression with robust standard errors and mediation analysis to test mechanisms.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first causal evidence on the political effects of hybrid news narratives, directly extending Iyengar's work on framing and polarization. It will clarify whether such blends can mitigate the trade-off between engagement and systemic understanding identified in earlier framing research, offering practical insights for journalistic practice and interventions against misinformation.
proposals.novelty: While episodic and thematic framing are well-studied in isolation, their interactive combination in a single narrative is a novel, ecologically valid stimulus. Testing its effects on both cognitive and affective outcomes bridges literatures on news framing and partisan animosity in a real-world electoral context.

proposals.title: The Cohesion-Pluralism Trade-off: How Partisan In-group Solidarity Undermines Respect for Democratic Out-groups
proposals.motivation: In an era of high affective polarization, strong partisan identity and in-group cohesion are often seen as natural features of democratic competition. However, pre-2026 research suggests that this cohesion may come at a cost to the pluralistic norms essential for a healthy democracy, such as tolerance and respect for political opponents. This project investigates the potential trade-off, examining whether the mechanisms that bind partisans together simultaneously erode the foundational respect for out-groups required for democratic stability.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that measures of partisan cohesiveness—including social homogeneity, message discipline, and shared affective responses to news—are positively associated with in-group loyalty but negatively associated with respect for the legitimacy and rights of opposing partisan groups.
proposals.method: We will combine a longitudinal panel survey around the 2024 U.S. election with a computational analysis of social media data. The survey will measure partisan social networks, affective polarization, and attitudes toward pluralistic norms. Concurrently, we will use NLP models (e.g., BERT-based classifiers) to analyze the content and emotional tone of political Facebook groups and Twitter/X communities linked to survey participants, quantifying in-group cohesiveness via semantic similarity and coordinated messaging. Time-series and multilevel regression models will test the relationship between these behavioral/language-based cohesion metrics and erosion of pluralistic attitudes.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first empirical test of the direct tension between partisan cohesion and pluralism, moving beyond documenting polarization to diagnosing a core democratic dilemma. It will contribute a novel methodological framework linking computational social science with traditional survey measures to understand the behavioral underpinnings of normative decline.
proposals.novelty: While existing work documents polarization and misinformation, the explicit framing of in-group cohesion as a potential *threat* to pluralism—and the integration of language-based cohesion metrics with longitudinal attitude data—represents a significant and non-obvious advance.

proposals.title: Beyond the Liberal Model: Testing the Generality of Affective Partisanship and Information Flows in Hybrid Regimes
proposals.motivation: Theories of affective polarization, partisan media, and misinformation diffusion are overwhelmingly developed and tested in stable liberal democracies like the United States. This creates a significant gap in understanding whether these dynamics are universal or are contingent on specific institutional and media contexts, such as those found in hybrid or illiberal democratic regimes where party systems may be weaker and state influence over media is stronger.
proposals.hypothesis: In hybrid regimes, affective polarization will be less tied to formal party identity and more to pro- vs. anti-regime cleavages, and the reach of (mis)information will be more constrained by state-aligned media architectures than by organic partisan sharing networks.
proposals.method: The study will employ a comparative, multi-method design. First, we will conduct panel surveys during national elections in two contrasting cases: Poland (a hybrid regime with a strong partisan divide) and Hungary (a more consolidated illiberal democracy). Surveys will measure affective polarization using feeling thermometers toward parties, leaders, and regime concepts. Second, we will replicate Iyengar et al.'s Facebook URL-sharing analysis framework for the same elections, mapping the diffusion networks of pro-government and oppositional content to compare their structure, reach, and susceptibility to misinformation against the U.S. 2020 baseline.
proposals.contribution: This research will provide a crucial test of the generalizability of core theories in political communication and psychology. It will generate novel empirical data on polarization and information ecosystems in understudied regimes and develop a more context-sensitive theoretical model that accounts for regime type as a key moderating variable.
proposals.novelty: While comparative studies exist, none have directly applied the specific measures of affective polarization and digital (mis)information diffusion, refined in the U.S. context by Iyengar and colleagues, to the distinct institutional and media landscapes of hybrid regimes.

proposals.title: Affective Polarization in Comparative Perspective: The Role of Electoral Systems and Media Environments
proposals.motivation: Research on affective polarization, where partisans dislike and distrust opposing party supporters, is heavily U.S.-centric. It remains unclear whether this phenomenon is a uniquely American pathology or a broader democratic challenge. A cross-national comparison is urgently needed to understand how institutional and media-system factors moderate partisan animosity.
proposals.hypothesis: Affective polarization will be more severe in majoritarian, two-party systems with highly commercialized and fragmented media markets, compared to proportional multi-party systems with strong public service broadcasters.
proposals.method: We will conduct a multi-wave panel survey during national election cycles in six democracies (e.g., U.S., UK, Germany, Sweden, Canada, Australia). The survey will measure affective polarization using standard feeling thermometers and social distance scales. We will link individual responses to country-level data on electoral systems (e.g., Gallagher Index) and media environments (e.g., PSB funding, market concentration). Analysis will employ hierarchical linear modeling (HLM) with time-series cross-sectional (TSCS) data to test institutional and media moderators on polarization trends.
proposals.contribution: This study will move beyond U.S.-specific diagnoses to provide a generalizable theory of affective polarization's drivers. It will directly inform debates about whether institutional reforms (e.g., electoral rules) or media policy can mitigate destructive partisan animosity, offering evidence-based insights for democratic resilience.
proposals.novelty: While prior work documents U.S. polarization, this project systematically tests its structural causes by comparing democracies with varying institutional designs, integrating political science and communication frameworks in a unified analysis.

proposals.title: Networked Narratives: Quantifying the Structural Cohesion of Partisan Discourse in the 2024 U.S. Election
proposals.motivation: Research on political polarization and misinformation has extensively documented partisan animosity and the spread of false claims, but has less often measured the internal coherence of partisan discourse itself. As the information value of traditional news declines and online platforms fragment communication, understanding the structural integrity of partisan narratives is crucial for diagnosing the health of democratic deliberation. This project addresses a key gap by moving beyond affective polarization to analyze the argumentative architecture of political communities.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that partisan discourse networks will exhibit high internal cohesion but low cross-partisan connectivity, and that the structural cohesion of a partisan network will be positively correlated with the affective polarization and partisan loyalty of its members.
proposals.method: We will collect a large corpus of text data (social media posts, partisan media transcripts, forum discussions) from the 2024 U.S. election period. Using pre-2026 natural language processing tools (e.g., BERT embeddings, topic modeling), we will construct semantic networks where nodes are key claims or narrative elements and edges represent co-occurrence or semantic similarity. We will apply network analysis metrics (e.g., modularity, clustering coefficient, average path length) to quantify the internal cohesion of Democratic and Republican discourse networks and the semantic distance between them.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide a novel, quantitative framework for measuring discourse cohesion, moving the field from descriptive accounts of polarization to structural analysis of narrative ecosystems. It will directly contribute to theories of affective polarization by testing its relationship to discursive structure and offer a new method for tracking the evolution and resilience of (mis)information narratives over time.
proposals.novelty: While network analysis has been used to map diffusion or social connections, applying it to model the semantic structure of partisan *arguments* as a cohesive system is a novel synthesis of Iyengar's work on polarization with computational discourse analysis. This approach quantifies a previously qualitative concept—narrative coherence—within distinct partisan spheres.

proposals.title: Measuring Rhetorical Hostility and Respect: A Sentiment Analysis Framework for Partisan Discourse and Democratic Health
proposals.motivation: Research by Iyengar and others demonstrates persistent affective polarization, where partisan animosity threatens democratic norms and deliberative discourse. However, current sentiment analysis tools, often trained on commercial or general-purpose corpora, are ill-suited to capture the nuanced expressions of respect, contempt, or dehumanization specific to political rhetoric. This gap limits our ability to systematically track the tone of partisan communication and its consequences for democratic pluralism.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a domain-adapted sentiment analysis model, trained on annotated political text, will reveal that elite partisan rhetoric has become significantly more hostile and less respectful over time, and that exposure to such hostile rhetoric, as measured by this model, is a stronger predictor of anti-democratic attitudes than exposure to merely negative or disagreeable content.
proposals.method: We will collect a large corpus of partisan text from congressional speeches, cable news transcripts, and major party social media accounts (2010-2024). Using a team of human coders, we will annotate a subset for a novel scheme: 'respectful disagreement,' 'neutral/issue-based,' 'mild hostility' (ad hominem), and 'severe hostility' (dehumanization). We will fine-tune a pre-2026 transformer-based model (e.g., BERT or RoBERTa) on this annotated data, moving beyond simple positive/negative valence. The trained model will then be applied longitudinally and linked to panel survey data on democratic attitudes.
proposals.contribution: This project will provide a validated, open-source computational tool for measuring rhetorical hostility in political science and communication research. It will produce novel longitudinal evidence on the intensification of disrespect in elite discourse and empirically test the link between exposure to model-classified hostile rhetoric and the erosion of support for pluralistic norms.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in moving sentiment analysis beyond commercial valence to operationalize theoretically grounded constructs of respect and hostility central to democratic theory, and in directly linking these computational measures to individual-level attitudes about democracy.

proposals.title: The Social Fabric of Corruption: Mapping Relational Networks in Media-Policing Exchanges
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 research on political corruption often frames it as a purely economic transaction, neglecting the social and relational contexts that enable and sustain it. This is critical in media-policing systems, where exchanges of favorable coverage for protection or information can undermine democratic accountability and public trust. Understanding these embedded networks is essential to addressing systemic corruption and its impact on polarization and misinformation.
proposals.hypothesis: Corrupt exchanges between media actors and police officials are not merely economic but are embedded in and reinforced by durable social and professional networks, which predict the persistence and geographic diffusion of such practices.
proposals.method: We will construct multiplex network models using public records, FOIA-obtained communication logs, and co-occurrence data from local news reports (2015-2024). Nodes represent individuals and organizations; edges capture professional ties, co-coverage events, and inferred relational proximity. Using stochastic block modeling and temporal exponential random graph models (TERGM), we will test for homophily and reciprocity within and across media-police clusters, analyzing network stability around election cycles and high-profile incidents.
proposals.contribution: This study will move corruption research beyond principal-agent models by empirically mapping the relational infrastructure of media-policing systems. It will contribute to theories of institutional corruption by integrating network science with political communication, showing how social ties facilitate exchanges that distort information flows and erode democratic norms.
proposals.novelty: While Iyengar's prior work examines media effects and polarization, this project uniquely applies network analysis to the covert relational substrate of corruption, treating media-policing exchanges as a networked system rather than isolated incidents. It bridges the study of misinformation with the structural analysis of corrupt institutions.

proposals.title: Chilling Effects: A Longitudinal Analysis of News Content and Journalist Self-Censorship Following Targeted Violence
proposals.motivation: The murder of journalists, such as Jamal Khashoggi in 2018, represents a severe threat to democratic accountability and information integrity. In a pre-2026 context of rising global authoritarianism and documented declines in hard news content, it is critical to empirically measure how such violent acts create a 'chilling effect,' altering journalistic output and public discourse beyond the immediate incident. This study addresses a gap in understanding the precise temporal and substantive contours of self-censorship as a response to targeted violence.
proposals.hypothesis: Following a high-profile journalist murder, news organizations and individual journalists will exhibit increased self-censorship, operationalized by a measurable decline in the frequency and critical tone of coverage related to the perpetrator(s) and associated issues, and this effect will be more pronounced for journalists geographically or thematically proximate to the victim.
proposals.method: We will employ a computational text analysis design, combining difference-in-differences and interrupted time series models. For a selected case (e.g., Khashoggi), we will collect a longitudinal corpus of articles from a global panel of news outlets (via LexisNexis/NewsBank) and journalist tweets (via Twitter API v1.1) for 12 months pre- and post-incident. Using pre-2026 NLP tools (BERT for topic modeling, VADER and fine-tuned sentiment classifiers for tone, dictionary methods for risk-associated keywords), we will quantify changes in content attributes. We will compare treatment groups (e.g., Saudi-focused journalists, international press) to control groups (journalists covering unrelated topics).
proposals.contribution: This research will provide the first large-scale, longitudinal, and behavioral evidence of self-censorship dynamics following journalist murders, moving beyond anecdotal accounts. It will contribute to political communication literature on the structural constraints of news production and to democratic theory by quantifying how violence distorts the information ecosystem.
proposals.novelty: While prior work examines media under pressure, this study uniquely isolates a specific, extreme shock (murder) and tracks its diffuse, behavioral consequences over time using digital trace data from both institutional and individual journalistic actors, bridging macro-level content analysis with micro-level behavioral measurement.

proposals.title: The Price of Pressure: Quantifying the Financial Impact of Extortion and Bribery on Media Outlets During Electoral Crises
proposals.motivation: In an era of heightened political polarization and democratic backsliding, the financial coercion of media outlets represents a critical, understudied threat to the information ecosystem. Pre-2026 research on misinformation and media economics has largely focused on audience demand and partisan supply, neglecting how direct financial pressure from political or criminal actors can distort news content and undermine institutional trust during high-stakes events like elections.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that during periods of acute political crisis (e.g., contested elections), media outlets that report critically on powerful actors will experience measurable, anomalous financial losses—through withdrawn advertising, canceled subscriptions, or regulatory harassment—compared to outlets that adopt a more accommodating editorial stance.
proposals.method: We will construct a panel dataset of U.S. local and national media outlets' financial indicators (ad revenue, subscription data from SEC filings and industry reports) and editorial content (via automated text analysis of coverage tone and framing) across the 2020 and 2024 election cycles. Using a difference-in-differences design combined with instrumental variable regression (using exogenous variation in local political pressure), we will isolate the causal effect of critical reporting on financial outcomes. We will supplement this with a survey experiment to measure audience intentions to financially punish outlets based on framed reporting of election disputes.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first systematic, causal evidence of how financial extortion operates as a mechanism of media capture in democratic contexts. It will extend the literature on affective polarization and misinformation by introducing a supply-side, economic constraint model, directly linking political pressure to the financial viability of news organizations.
proposals.novelty: While existing work documents partisan media bias and audience polarization, this project uniquely focuses on the direct financial coercion of media *as an outcome*, moving beyond content analysis to quantify the economic costs of editorial independence during crises.

proposals.title: The Chilling Effect: How Self-Censorship After Political Violence Shapes Public Knowledge and Affective Polarization
proposals.motivation: In the pre-2026 context, rising political violence and threats against public figures (e.g., the 2017 Congressional baseball shooting, threats to election officials post-2020) create a climate of fear that may induce self-censorship among journalists and ordinary citizens. This research addresses a critical gap in understanding how this behavioral adaptation, distinct from top-down censorship, alters the information ecosystem, public knowledge, and the affective foundations of partisan animosity central to democratic health.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that self-censorship following salient acts of political violence creates localized 'information deserts,' which in turn (a) reduce factual knowledge about the triggering event and related issues, and (b) increase affective polarization as in-group allegiances and speculative narratives fill the informational void.
proposals.method: We will employ a multi-wave panel survey experiment, fielded around a future real-world triggering event (e.g., an act of political violence). Using a geolocated sample, we will measure self-reported willingness to discuss politics and share news (self-censorship) and combine this with digital trace data (via consented Facebook URL sharing history) to validate behavioral silence. We will implement a difference-in-differences design, comparing knowledge and affective polarization (feeling thermometers, trait ratings) over time between high-self-censorship and low-self-censorship networks, controlling for prior attitudes, media diet, and demographic factors.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first longitudinal, behavioral evidence linking self-censorship to measurable shifts in public knowledge and political affect. It will extend theories of affective polarization by identifying information suppression as a novel causal mechanism and contribute to democratic theory by quantifying a subtle, bottom-up form of information distortion distinct from algorithmic or institutional censorship.
proposals.novelty: While prior Iyengar work has examined the diffusion of misinformation and the durability of polarization, this study uniquely focuses on the *absence* of information flow due to fear-induced behavioral change, and its downstream attitudinal consequences, a causal pathway not yet empirically mapped.

proposals.title: Targeting the Fourth Estate: A Political Event Data Analysis of Violence Against Journalists and Democratic Accountability
proposals.motivation: The integrity of democratic information systems is under threat from rising violence and harassment targeting journalists, a trend documented by pre-2026 reports from organizations like the Committee to Protect Journalists. This violence directly undermines the news media's ability to inform the public, a core concern in Iyengar's work on the declining information value of news and the diffusion of misinformation. Understanding the systematic political and electoral patterns behind these attacks is crucial for diagnosing threats to democratic accountability.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that violent events targeting journalists, as coded in political event datasets, will systematically increase in frequency and severity in the lead-up to competitive national elections and will be strategically concentrated in regions with high levels of pre-existing political polarization and partisan media ecosystems.
proposals.method: We will leverage established political event data projects (e.g., GDELT, ACLED) to extract and analyze events involving violence, threats, or harassment against journalists globally from 2015-2024. Using time-series regression models and conflict forecasting techniques (e.g., gradient boosted trees, LASSO regression for feature selection), we will test the correlation between these events and electoral calendars, measures of affective polarization (drawn from survey data), and the local reach of hyper-partisan or misinformation-laden Facebook content as mapped in prior research. The analysis will control for general conflict levels and institutional factors.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first large-N, systematic evidence linking electoral cycles and affective polarization to direct violence against the press, moving beyond case studies. It will extend Iyengar's research on polarization and misinformation by showing how these forces may manifest in physical attacks on information producers. The findings will offer a new metric for assessing the health of democratic information environments.
proposals.novelty: While event data is commonly used for conflict forecasting, its application to systematically track violence against a specific professional group—journalists—in explicit relation to electoral timing and affective polarization is novel. It bridges the literature on political violence, communication, and democratic erosion in a quantifiable way.

proposals.title: The Chilling Effect in Real Time: How a High-Profile Journalist Kidnapping Alters News Content and Polarization in U.S. Media
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 research on media and polarization often focuses on systemic, long-term trends or election-specific misinformation. However, a critical gap exists in understanding how singular, traumatic events within the media ecosystem itself—like the kidnapping of a prominent journalist—can cause immediate, industry-wide shifts in news practices and content. This matters because such exogenous shocks could rapidly alter the information environment, potentially accelerating trends of partisan framing or risk-averse reporting, with direct consequences for democratic discourse.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a high-profile journalist kidnapping will induce a measurable chilling effect, causing national news outlets to reduce the substantive depth of reporting on sensitive topics (e.g., corruption, conflict) and increase the use of partisan-affective language as they navigate audience expectations and perceived threats, thereby temporarily amplifying affective polarization in related news coverage.
proposals.method: We will employ a longitudinal, multi-method design centered on a detailed case study (e.g., the 2014 kidnapping of James Foley). Using pre-2025 NLP tools, we will perform time-series analysis on a large corpus of U.S. broadcast and digital news transcripts (from databases like LexisNexis and TV News Archive) from 6 months before to 12 months after the incident. We will measure changes in: 1) substantive information density via keyword and topic modeling (LDA), 2) affective partisan language using Lexicoder Sentiment Dictionary and custom partisan frame dictionaries, and 3) network diffusion patterns of related stories on Facebook (using CrowdTangle data, pre-2026 access) to assess reach and engagement polarization.
proposals.contribution: This study will contribute a novel, event-centered framework for understanding rapid media evolution, moving beyond slow-moving trends. It will provide concrete, causal-like evidence of how threats to journalists directly shape the informational content available to the public. The findings will bridge literature on media economics, political communication, and democratic erosion by quantifying a specific mechanism—the chilling effect—through which violence impacts public discourse.
proposals.novelty: While prior work by Iyengar and others tracks declining news substance or persistent polarization, this proposal uniquely isolates a discrete, exogenous shock as a catalyst for change, applying rigorous time-series and content analysis to trace its ripple effects across the entire media sector in near-real time.

proposals.title: The Micro-Dynamics of Information Resilience: How Citizen Journalists Circumvent State Media Suppression
proposals.motivation: In an era of increasing state control over traditional media and the documented decline of its information value, understanding how critical information flows under suppression is vital for democratic resilience. This project addresses a core gap in the study of misinformation and polarization by shifting focus from the diffusion of falsehoods to the grassroots production and verification of factual reporting in hostile environments.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that during periods of acute state media suppression, citizen journalists will form decentralized, trust-based networks for information verification, but that the content they produce will exhibit higher affective polarization and lower institutional attribution compared to traditional professional reporting.
proposals.method: We will conduct a large-scale, longitudinal analysis of social media data (e.g., Twitter, Telegram) from a case study of a major protest event in an authoritarian context (e.g., Hong Kong 2019-2020, Belarus 2020). Using a combination of network analysis to map information-sharing clusters, natural language processing (specifically BERT-based topic modeling and sentiment analysis) to characterize content, and time-series regression, we will measure changes in network structure, informational themes, and affective language before, during, and after state crackdowns on traditional media.
proposals.contribution: This research will contribute a novel, individual-level framework for studying information ecosystems under duress, moving beyond institutional analysis. It will empirically map the trade-offs between information resilience and content quality/polarization, offering critical insights for policymakers and civil society organizations focused on supporting independent media.
proposals.novelty: While existing work examines the reach of misinformation on platforms or broad polarization trends, this study uniquely combines network science and NLP to trace the *production* and *professionalization* of citizen-sourced news at scale, directly testing how suppression alters the fundamental sociology of news gathering.

proposals.title: The Agenda-Setting Shield: How Partisan Media Framing Shapes Public Perceptions of Police Corruption
proposals.motivation: Public trust in law enforcement is a cornerstone of democratic legitimacy, yet perceptions of police corruption vary widely. Pre-2026 research on media and policing focuses on how police actions influence coverage. This study flips the lens, examining how journalistic framing, particularly in a polarized media ecosystem, actively constructs these perceptions. This is critical for understanding the roots of political polarization over institutions and the potential for media-driven erosion of democratic accountability.
proposals.hypothesis: Exposure to partisan-framed news reports about police misconduct significantly influences individuals' perceptions of police corruption, with effects moderated by the viewer's partisan identity and the affective polarization of the media source.
proposals.method: We will employ a multi-wave panel survey experiment (N~2000) combined with computational text analysis. Participants will be randomly assigned to view real, decontextualized news clips about police incidents, sourced from outlets with known partisan leanings (e.g., Fox News, MSNBC). We will measure pre- and post-exposure corruption perceptions. The text of the clips will be analyzed using pre-2026 NLP tools (e.g., LIWC for moral framing, custom dictionaries for blame attribution) to quantify framing features. Analysis will use hierarchical linear modeling to assess the interaction between media frame, source partisanship, and participant partisanship on perception shifts.
proposals.contribution: This research will provide the first causal evidence for media as an independent driver of institutional trust in the policing domain, moving beyond correlational studies. It will extend theories of affective and partisan media effects into the realm of non-electoral, authority-based institutions. The findings will inform debates on journalistic responsibility and the democratic consequences of polarized news narratives.
proposals.novelty: While agenda-setting is well-studied for political candidates, its application to perceptions of systemic institutional corruption like policing is novel. The study's core innovation is testing the *inverse* of the standard model—not how police corruption shapes news, but how news shapes the perception of corruption—within the established pre-2026 framework of partisan media ecosystems.

ideas.title: Applying Ecological Niche Analysis to Media Framing Across Formats
ideas.description: This research adapts the problem-solving approach from ecology, specifically niche theory and analysis, to the study of media framing. In ecology, scientists determine how species coexist by measuring their use of shared resources (their niche) within an environment. Analogously, this study treats episodic and thematic frames as cognitive resources and print/television as distinct informational environments. The analogous solution—using quantitative methods to map resource use, niche breadth, and overlap—is adapted by conducting a synchronized content analysis of print and TV coverage for identical political issues. Key metrics would measure the 'frame niche' of each format (e.g., the proportion and combination of frames used) and the 'niche overlap' between formats. The goal is to systematically model how the inherent constraints of each media format (e.g., TV's temporal flow and visual emphasis vs. print's spatial layout and textual depth) create distinct selective pressures that lead to the specialization, dominance, or exclusion of certain framing strategies, explaining the observed differences in framing across media.

ideas.title: Bayesian Framing: Modeling Journalistic Frame Selection as Inference Under Public Priors
ideas.description: This study adapts the solution to the inverse problem in Bayesian perception to the domain of media framing. In perception, the brain uses prior expectations to interpret ambiguous sensory data. Analogously, journalists face an 'inverse problem' of selecting a coherent narrative frame from complex event data. The research posits that they solve this by performing a form of (often intuitive) Bayesian inference, where pre-existing public attributions of responsibility for an issue act as a powerful prior probability distribution. The adapted idea is to formally model this process, using computational methods to test how the strength and valence of measured public responsibility 'priors' predict the probability of journalists selecting corresponding frames, thereby providing a formal mechanism for how public sentiment shapes media narratives.

ideas.title: Agent-Based Modeling of Cynicism Propagation Driven by Automated Frame Detection in Media
ideas.description: This research integrates computational linguistics and agent-based modeling to study how media framing dynamically influences political behavior. Automated NLP systems classify thematic (systemic) and episodic (individual) frames in real-time news transcripts, quantifying the 'dose' of cynicism-inducing content. This data then drives a large-scale agent-based model where agents, embedded in social networks, process these frames, adjust their internal cynicism levels, and influence each other, ultimately affecting simulated voter turnout. The synergy enables testing counterfactual scenarios, such as how altering the proportion of thematic frames in news coverage might curb cynicism propagation and increase electoral participation.

ideas.title: Temporal Transcriptomics: A Molecular Biology Approach to Broadcast Frame Evolution
ideas.description: This research adapts the core methodology of temporal transcriptomics from molecular biology to the study of media framing. In transcriptomics, a time-series analysis of RNA sequencing reveals the dynamic cascade of gene expression in response to a stimulus. Here, we perform a micro-analysis of a single major news broadcast, treating it as a unified 'response' to a news event. The broadcast is segmented into a dense time series (e.g., every 2 minutes). Each segment is coded for dominant narrative frames, metaphorical language, sourcing, and emotional valence. The resulting data is not a static snapshot but a dynamic 'expression profile' of the broadcast, visually and quantitatively mapping how frames are introduced, compete, hybridize, and succeed one another across the timeline. This reveals the editorial 'cascade'—the internal logic and temporal dependencies of framing decisions as the story is built and presented in real-time.

ideas.title: Framing Effects on Policy Preferences: An Analogical Adaptation from Neuroimaging Decoding
ideas.description: This research adapts a solution from cognitive neuroscience—multivariate pattern analysis (MVPA) used to decode cognitive states from brain activity—to the study of media framing effects. The analogous problem in neuroscience involves isolating a specific signal (e.g., the pattern for 'face perception') from complex, noisy data. Here, we aim to isolate the distinct 'signature' of episodic (personal story-driven) and thematic (broader context-driven) news frames on public policy preferences. The adapted method involves exposing participants to policy news stories with controlled episodic or thematic frames, while collecting a multi-modal suite of dependent measures: self-reported policy support, skin conductance response, facial electromyography for micro-expressions, reaction times on implicit association tests, and open-ended thought listings. A machine learning classifier (e.g., SVM) will then be trained on this multi-dimensional response data to predict which frame a participant saw. Successfully 'decoding' the frame from the response pattern will precisely identify the constellation of affective, cognitive, and attitudinal changes uniquely triggered by each frame, moving beyond simple mean comparisons to model the complex, interactive pathways of framing effects on policy preferences.

ideas.title: Multimodal Framing Analysis of Short-Form News Video on Digital Platforms
ideas.description: This research develops and applies a Multimodal Framing Analysis (MFA) framework to short-form news videos on platforms like TikTok, Instagram Reels, and YouTube Shorts. It systematically codes for framing devices not only in spoken or on-screen text but also in visual composition (imagery, overlays, source appearance), audio cues (music, tone, sound effects), and editorial techniques (pacing, cuts, transitions). By analyzing how these modalities interact to construct and amplify specific frames, the study captures the holistic persuasive strategy of the format. The output is a robust model for understanding how news narratives are engineered for engagement in algorithmically-driven, attention-competitive video environments.

ideas.title: Causal Inference Framework for Disentangling Frame Propagation from Shared Latent Drivers
ideas.description: This research develops a system to distinguish genuine frame diffusion between media outlets from spurious correlations caused by common external shocks or shared latent biases. It constructs dynamic, time-stamped networks of frame co-occurrence and applies causal inference methods from econometrics and complex systems to estimate the direction and strength of influence pathways. The model incorporates potential instrumental variables (e.g., geographic constraints, exclusive source access) to control for confounders. The output is a causal influence network that quantifies the probability of direct frame propagation versus independent adoption, providing a more robust account of how narratives actually spread.

ideas.title: Applying Paleoclimatological Core Sampling to Media Framing Analysis
ideas.description: This research adapts the core sampling methodology from paleoclimatology to the study of longitudinal changes in media framing. In paleoclimatology, scientists drill ice cores and analyze layered deposits using chemical proxies to reconstruct past climate trends and shifts. Analogously, this study will treat a digitized, longitudinal news archive as a temporal 'core.' It will implement systematic sampling at regular intervals (e.g., weekly samples from a decades-long archive). For each sample, Natural Language Processing (NLP) and frame analysis techniques will be used to quantify key proxies—specifically, the prevalence and ratio of episodic versus thematic frames for a chosen issue. The resulting time-series data will then be analyzed using spectral analysis and change-point detection models, analogous to climatological time-series analysis, to identify cyclical patterns (e.g., seasonal framing variations), abrupt shifts in narrative focus (analogous to climatic tipping points), and long-term secular trends in how the issue is framed publicly over extended periods.

ideas.title: Multimodal Computational Framework for Context-Aware Frame Detection in Broadcast News

It integrates natural language processing with computer vision (for on-screen text and graphics) and audio analysis (for prosody and speaker characteristics) to capture the complete rhetorical and contextual presentation of frames. This approach overcomes the inherent ambiguity and loss of information in text-only analysis, enabling more robust, valid, and fine-grained classification of episodic and thematic framing across large-scale video archives.

ideas.title: Adapting Epidemiological Compartmental Models to Map Frame Virality and Resistance in Media Networks
ideas.description: This idea adapts the core solution from epidemiology—compartmental modeling of disease spread—to the research context of frame diffusion in media. In the analogous field, models like SIR (Susceptible-Infected-Recovered) are applied to networks to predict and analyze outbreak dynamics. The adapted research idea applies this framework by modeling media outlets and commentators as a network where narrative frames behave like pathogens. Nodes are assigned states (e.g., Susceptible to a frame, Infected by it, or Resistant to it), and the transition probabilities between states are parameterized using network science metrics (e.g., edge weights based on citation patterns or shared audience data) and content analysis (e.g., ideological similarity). This approach allows researchers to move beyond static mapping to dynamic simulation, enabling predictions about which frames will become endemic, the threshold for narrative 'herd immunity,' and the critical points where counter-messaging might be most effective.

ideas.title: Measuring Behavioral Responsibility Through Consequential, Multi-Stage Experimental Games with Media Framing
ideas.description: This research program uses a longitudinal experimental design where participants are first exposed to controlled media frames about a societal issue. They then engage in a series of incentivized games that require costly, delayed, and socially observable actions—such as binding pledges of future earnings, irreversible allocations to real charities, or effortful information acquisition—to measure responsibility-taking behavior. The design isolates the causal effect of framing on these consequential behaviors, tracks persistence over time, and incorporates real-world outcome uncertainty to enhance ecological validity beyond abstract laboratory choices.

ideas.title: Testing for a Latent 'Political Apathy' Variable in the Cynicism-Turnout Link
ideas.description: This idea challenges the assumption that induced cynicism directly causes lower turnout by proposing the relationship may be spurious, confounded by a latent variable of generalized political apathy. Drawing an analogy from epidemiology's approach to untangling spurious correlations between risk factors and health outcomes, the adapted research solution is to explicitly model and measure the hypothesized confounder. The study would test if the cynicism-turnout link is mediated or moderated by pre-existing apathy (measured via political efficacy and locus of control), using causal inference methods to isolate any direct effect of cynicism, thereby clarifying the true behavioral mechanism.

ideas.title: Comparative Framing Genomics: A High-Throughput Pipeline for Global News Analysis
ideas.description: This idea adapts the solution from genomics and bioinformatics, where standardized, automated pipelines are used to analyze massive, diverse biological datasets, to the challenge of scaling up framing analysis across dozens of countries. The analogous genomic solution involves using common reference sequences for alignment and modular tools for specific analytical tasks to generate comparable data at scale. The adapted research idea is to create a 'Comparative Framing Genomics' pipeline: a standardized, largely automated computational workflow that ingests multilingual television news content, uses a common conceptual 'reference issue' framework (e.g., a detailed ontology of climate change subtopics) for alignment, runs a suite of specialized natural language processing modules to identify and quantify specific media frames, and finally produces a structured, harmonized dataset. This pipeline would enable a truly global, systematic, and reproducible comparative analysis of how issues like climate change are framed across national media ecosystems, overcoming the scalability limitations of manual or ad-hoc methodological approaches.

ideas.title: Applying Seismic Signal Pattern Recognition for Frame Detection in News Transcripts
ideas.description: This idea adapts a solution from geophysics to the problem of automated frame detection in news transcripts. In seismology, algorithms like STA/LTA are used to detect distinct seismic events within continuous, noisy waveform data by identifying characteristic signal patterns. Analogously, we treat a television news transcript as a continuous 'signal' of text. The adapted research idea involves developing a computational linguistics algorithm that scans this textual signal for sharp changes or distinctive patterns in a suite of linguistic features—such as sentiment polarity, density of specific topic keywords, pronoun ratios (I/we vs. they), and narrative tense markers. These detected patterns, analogous to seismic events, would then be used to automatically segment the transcript and classify each segment as employing either an episodic frame (characterized by concrete, event-specific language) or a thematic frame (characterized by abstract, contextual, and explanatory language), enabling large-scale analysis of framing in broadcast news.

ideas.title: Triangulating Visual Attention, Emotional Response, and Cognitive Encoding to Negative Political Ads
ideas.description: This research system simultaneously tracks voters' visual attention via eye-tracking, their covert emotional responses via facial EMG and skin conductance, and their immediate cognitive encoding via a rapid serial visual presentation (RSVP) recognition task for ad elements. By synchronizing these multimodal data streams, the design distinguishes between mere visual fixation and meaningful cognitive-emotional engagement. This allows researchers to identify not just what was looked at, but whether negative ad elements triggered aversion, anger, or agreement, and which specific claims or images were successfully encoded into memory.

ideas.title: Disentangling Pathways: Mapping the Cognitive Mechanisms by Which Thematic Frames Attenuate Responsibility
ideas.description: This research program moves beyond the simple hypothesis that thematic frames reduce responsibility to rigorously identify and test the specific psychological mechanisms responsible. Through an initial qualitative phase, it maps the distinct narratives and justifications people generate when using thematic explanations. It then designs experiments that manipulate frame elements to selectively activate proposed mediators like diffused agency, perceived intractability, or reduced personal efficacy. The outcome is a contingent, mechanistic model predicting when and why thematic framing leads to attenuated blame, rather than a monolithic effect.

ideas.title: Use a Polygraph-Style Triangulation Protocol for Negative Ad Response
ideas.description: This idea adapts the core methodological protocol from forensic psychophysiology (polygraphy) to the study of responses to negative political advertising. In polygraphy, a suspect's physiological responses to crime-relevant questions are systematically compared to their responses to control questions across electrodermal, cardiovascular, and respiratory channels to isolate deception-related arousal. Adapted here, participants would be exposed to a target negative political ad embedded within a sequence of control ads (e.g., neutral, positive). Using multi-channel biometric sensors (EDA, ECG, respiration), the research would not simply measure raw arousal during the negative ad, but would instead calculate a differential reactivity score for each channel by comparing the response to the negative ad against the individual's baseline response to the control ads. This triangulation method controls for individual differences in general physiological reactivity and aims to isolate a more precise psychophysiological signature specific to the negative emotional appeal, moving beyond simple arousal to a patterned response indicative of the ad's intended affective impact.

ideas.title: The Narrative Catalyst: Using Episodic Priming to Bridge to Systemic Policy Frames
ideas.description: This idea adapts the 'prime-boost' strategy from immunology to challenge the assumption that episodic and systemic frames are dichotomous. In vaccinology, a prime prepares the system for a more effective boost. Analogously, this research proposes that a powerful individual story (episodic prime) does not compete with systemic explanations but can instead cognitively and emotionally 'prime' an audience, creating receptivity and a felt need for explanation that a subsequent systemic policy frame (thematic boost) can effectively satisfy. The adapted research would experimentally test different sequences (episodic-then-systemic vs. systemic-then-episodic vs. combined) and temporal delays to map the conditions under which an episodic narrative most effectively catalyzes demand for systemic solutions, effectively treating narrative engagement as a preparatory catalyst for policy persuasion.

ideas.title: Modeling Ad-Driven Cynicism as an Epidemic of Social Contagion
ideas.description: This research adapts the classic epidemic modeling solution from epidemiology to study the propagation of ad-driven cynicism. In the analogous field, compartmental models (e.g., SIR) within agent-based frameworks simulate disease spread through networks based on transmission rules. The adapted idea models cynicism as a socially contagious state. Agents in a simulated voter network transition between states (e.g., Trusting, Cynical, Resistant) based on exposure to political advertising and peer influence, with parameters for 'transmission probability' and 'recovery rate' calibrated from survey data. This approach allows for simulating the 'epidemic curve' of cynicism, testing how network topology and intervention strategies (e.g., fact-checking as a 'vaccination') affect the ultimate outbreak size of disengagement and its causal impact on voter turnout dynamics.

ideas.title: Narrative Bridging: Testing Episodic-to-Systemic Transference Against Compassion Collapse
ideas.description: This research program directly confronts the critical failure mode where powerful individual stories backfire, narrowing support to the featured case alone. It employs a series of controlled experiments and longitudinal surveys to identify the specific narrative and contextual 'bridges'—such as explicit causal linkage to policy, pluralization of examples, or diagnostic framing—that reliably channel empathy from an episodic account toward systemic reform. The outcome is a robust, conditional theory of narrative efficacy that specifies when stories catalyze broad demand and when they trigger compassion collapse, providing actionable guidance for communicators.

ideas.title: A Benchmark-Anchored Computational System for Tracking Longitudinal Frame Prevalence
ideas.description: This study tracks shifts in episodic vs. thematic media framing over time using a hybrid human-computational system to ensure measurement consistency. A manually coded, temporally distributed benchmark corpus defines the frames and is used to train and validate a transparent NLP model for large-scale analysis. The model's outputs on the full longitudinal corpus are periodically audited against the human benchmark to detect and correct for any conceptual drift in the automated coding. This approach combines the scalability of computational methods with the conceptual rigor of manual coding to produce robust, reliable longitudinal trends.

ideas.title: Precision Immunosuppression for Political Demobilization
ideas.description: This research adapts the solution from immunology's targeted drug delivery to the problem of testing demobilization via digital ads. In immunotherapy, precision targeting uses ligands to deliver suppressive drugs directly to specific overactive immune cells. The analogous solution here is to engineer micro-targeted negative ads as 'suppressive payloads' bound to 'psychographic ligands'—ad creative and targeting parameters designed to engage only a precise voter segment. The study would test if this scaled-down, high-precision approach induces demobilization (e.g., reduced intent to vote) in the targeted group more effectively than broad negative messaging, while measuring spillover to untargeted groups as a key containment metric, analogous to monitoring systemic side effects in medicine.

ideas.title: Computational Micro-Targeting: Using NLP to Dynamically Optimize Negative Ad Tone for Demobilization
ideas.description: This research fuses computational text analysis with micro-targeted ad experiments to move beyond testing if negative ads demobilize, to discover how they demobilize. By applying NLP to the transcripts of thousands of personalized digital ad variants, we can measure tonal dimensions at a scale and precision impossible for human coders. This allows for a large-scale field experiment where ad tone is systematically varied and targeted, enabling causal inference about which precise linguistic features drive demobilization effects in different demographic and psychographic micro-groups.

ideas.title: A Human-Validated, Construct-Grounded Framework for Computational Tone Analysis in Advertising
ideas.description: This system addresses the validity gap in automated tone analysis by formally integrating theoretical communication constructs with scalable NLP. It employs an iterative loop: NLP proposes a broad feature space from ad transcripts, communication theory experts define key constructs and code a strategically sampled subset of ads, and these human judgments are used to train and validate constrained, interpretable models that measure each construct. The final output is a scalable, auditable measurement tool where each automated tone score is explicitly grounded in and validated against human coding of its theoretical definition.

ideas.title: Multimodal measurement of visual and cognitive frame attention using eye-tracking and EEG
ideas.description: This research system employs synchronized eye-tracking and electroencephalography (EEG) to overcome the limitation of measuring only overt visual attention. By capturing neural signatures of covert attention, such as the P300 component, it identifies which visual elements viewers are cognitively processing even when not directly fixated upon. This allows for a more complete map of attentional allocation across a visual frame, distinguishing between simple gaze location and active cognitive engagement. The integrated data provides a robust, dual-measurement approach to understanding how viewers perceive and process complex visual scenes.

ideas.title: Use visual saliency heatmaps from computer vision to quantify frame attention
ideas.description: This idea adapts a solution from computer vision to the problem of measuring visual frame attention. In computer vision, visual saliency algorithms analyze static images or video frames to predict where human attention will be drawn based on computational models of low-level image features like contrast, color, and edges, producing a saliency heatmap. This solution is adapted by applying these pre-existing computational saliency models (e.g., Itti-Koch, GBVS) directly to the visual frames or scenes under study. The adapted research idea uses these algorithmically generated saliency heatmaps as a predicted map of visual attention for each frame. This provides a scalable, hardware-free method to quantify the inherent attentional pull of visual elements, which can then be validated against or contrasted with empirical eye-tracking data from psychology to disentangle bottom-up stimulus-driven attention from top-down viewer-driven factors.
